speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Jean Wilson Ringsage and Steiner Ringsage took place at their home in Park on October 5th, 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: The final bit where the cord isn't enclosed in the spinal column. There's usually a sac and if it breaks, meningitis sets in and that's it. And mongoloids and other abnormal cases. Maybe only 10 -12 babies like that all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that the place you worked like all the time? Most or most of the time?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well I worked in a maternity ward for about, in St. Vincent's for about, in St. Vincent's for about 9 months one time. I liked that too. But I liked this much better. Much more interesting job. Then I had the well babies. Perfectly normal babies but there were about 50 % of them up from adoption from broken homes or unwed mothers or whatever. And then there was children brought in there, babies brought in there, born, well a mother was taken out of the penitentiary in order to give birth to the baby in a hospital and brought up there. I'll remember what I had. Lovely, beautiful, healthy baby boy. Ten days before he was born his mother had killed his father with an axe.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And his grandmother or somebody in the family took him and we had these poor little battered things that were all beat up by their fathers, fathers or boyfriends. And mothers would go insane or have tuberculosis or something like that and we'd keep them until somebody was able to take care of them but it was a very, very lovely establishment. And then we had a room full of boarders, people that wanted to go on a holiday somewhere that could afford, they were paying guests. They were paying, well they didn't get any better care in a way than the other ones but they wanted to go to Europe or some place and they wanted to be absolutely sure the child was taken care of. So I was there and they were very nice, they were very mean and my daughters had babies between time and they always gave us plenty of time to take care of them. They were good but it was always interesting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: These babies, these little children that were defective, did you care for them too?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes, I was there about, I guess I was five years old then, nights and then days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did you think of working with them?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: You just, you're not conscious of being defective after maybe the first week or so, you just love them to death. You just love them and weep over them, you talk to them. You don't weep when they die, yes, but you're happy when you're with them. It's really a special thing. You see a child, his little spine is open, maybe it will only live a very short time, and you realize that I am his whole lifetime. I'm the day nurse, he's possibly sleeping at night. What I say, what I do, he's just like a fresh page, this is the first page in a book that's never going to be finished. And I, what I think, what I say, it's a tremendous discipline on you to control your thoughts because they're conscious of everything you're thinking, I'm sure of it. It's just, that's the way I feel about babies. When you get older you have the tense of sense blurred, but a mother, or a substitute mother, her thoughts and her emotions are very important to that baby. There's just a constant communication between you, and there's conversation going on, we were always encouraged to talk to them. And I remember one, he had a huge head and they'd operate it, and I thought I was so sorry for the parents because it was such a heartbreak to come and see them and then go, but he was such an appreciated baby. There were some babies that are so appreciative of everything you do, you just pat them and feed them and talk to them, their whole body responds, then you know that they know that you're talking to them. Some of them, their intelligence is just great. Others are a little below, the little mongoloids are happy little things. They're little elves, they're little eyes like you, they're the happiest ones. The lesson was, they're hard preps on the parents, but to take care of them, they respond. They were only up to about a year old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you didn't find it emotionally draining?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, no it wasn't. Because these things happen, and I found it was good discipline for me to keep my mind going in a certain way, in a certain channel, to fill that baby's days with constructive thoughts and happy thoughts, tell them about the world, the birds or what I saw, or communicate. When he died, or she died, well that was it. But you always had that feeling that I'm important to that baby. Not only physically taking care of him, but because in that child there's a record that there won't be an adult.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you feel the same way about your children?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, no I didn't because I always felt they'd grow up, but I knew that child was going to die. I knew it's time was limited. It just mattered maybe weeks, sometimes months. No, no I had quite a different feeling because I knew my impressions were limited, but them, I'm sure, are just about the same as any other mother. Certainly I had a certain influence. But all things that would come into the world would fade out or dilute. But they're different. They're very different. I felt sorry and sad that parents weren't experiencing what I was experiencing with them. I don't, they couldn't, they were too emotional, there was too much heartbreak. I could be on a different level.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think these kids themselves had a different, their awareness was heightened because of this?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, I don't think it was particularly heightened one way or the other. I think they had different levels of intelligence. Some were perfectly normal, perfectly normal responses, and others were subnormal, but sweet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the name of the place by the way in Portland?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: It's Our Lady of Providence Nursery. It has been completely changed since I've been there and it's going all together into children that are defective. There was a good, there was about 90 % of them normal when I was there. Just this one room. But now there's just so many more, there seems to be, of course a bigger population. There's some had rare diseases that call for special diets. Well, Chris wanted to talk to you about the early days. Yes, well that was just, well you let me start down there. No, that's interesting. Yes, and there's one or two babies I could tell you about that were above and beyond what you'd expect in a relationship. There was something there. But we can go on to what you came out here for.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For our early days in our lives, would it be our married lives? Or before that when I was a girl?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I guess, firstly I was wanting to know about you when you were a kid.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And little girls?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes, and this isolation that you grew up in. Well, my mother came out from the east, Prince Edward Island. She talked for five years, she said that's enough, I'm going out. Her brother was working on the Okanogan, the Kootenai Lakes. And there was a tie of some kind down here. So she came but she settled in Belmore. And there was an opening, she had found some friends there and there was an opening. She just worked whatever she could find and that happened to be sort of a... She helped upstairs and I guess she helped maybe wait a little and she helped with the books and managed in this small hotel. And I guess upstairs she met my father and then he was fairly prosperous then. And he had been a miner. I mean he had mining claims and he had timber claims and gone out and worked at mapping out timber claims for companies. When they put in that railroad north of the CNR, he worked all in through that area getting the timber claims ready for when the railroad didn't come in well. In due time I arrived and I was born in a log cabin that belonged to the manager of a mine. That's another thing, Daddy was very versatile. They were opening a coal mine and he was there and he was the one supervising that. And then we moved to Belmore and that was next to that place that had that terrific Frank slide where the mountain came down. I told you last time the month of my aunt had a mother had a sister come out. She was married then. This aunt was being courted by this young tailor and he left his tailor shop and came up there and spent the night visiting her, the evening visiting her and then went to a hotel. And during that night his tailor shop was completely covered by this mountain. The whole town was just covered.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the town of Frank?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, it was the town of Frank. You can look it up in history. You can see it now. I think there was maybe 700 or 800 people. I don't know how many people. There was a bank.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were many people killed?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes, there was quite a few killed. I used to work in the mines and I worked with a guy that was down in there at the time. He and his partner, I remember they had a bottle of whiskey up there and it was two o 'clock. And he said, let's go up and get that. They put in their work. They've done their work. He said, let's hurry and get up. And once they heard the thunder, you know when rocks start to fall it sounds like thunder. And so they waited a while and finally they got word that the mountain tipped over. So they couldn't get out. And it took them, I forget, three days and three nights to make something like that. To make a hole through it. And I said to Jeff and JB, he says, we wasn't hungry. He said, oh we want to just hurry up. Come on down, you're tired. Let me dig.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: See?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Get down till they got right up through there. And then day and night they just worked till they got up through.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they dug up through the rubble through the whole thing?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Rock and all. I think it was 30 something feet. Oh it is tremendous. Oh it is just tremendous. You see the whole mountain. It's just a huge hole. I've walked over the rocks. I don't know what I've had explain to me. There's a lot of rocks that got undermined or something. It's part of the Rockies, you know. It's for crow's nest. Well anyhow that happened and so he made a fresh start then in this little town of Blammer. But we left there when I was about two and a half.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your father lose anything in that?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No he didn't. He wasn't in no money involved. It was about four or five miles away. Something like that from Blammer. And then we went up to the next town. Crow's nest pass was called Coleman. That was about seven or eight miles away. And daddy took a homestead up on the mountain. We just wanted for cattle grazing straight up. Just about like being on the top. The town would be like below one of these canyons here. And then we were on top. Oh I guess it would be about a couple of miles. You could look down and see the town. It was Coleman. And then we went up to the next town. It was about five. Coleman. They had a good sized mine going there. They had coke mines in those days. You could see these great big coke mines. It was quite spectacular at night. In the day it was dreary looking. He wouldn't consent to live in. He just picked people that lived in the town. He was a character. He just ran away from home. He was about thirteen or fourteen. He never went home again. Never rose. Never communicated.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who is this?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: My dad. Oh yeah. That's why I never go in. I know a little bit about his background. But. And wandered and did pretty good. Well. We stayed there in Coleman. Up in the mountain. Mother would visit. She would get out. She went back to Prince Edward Island. Had a little baby boy that died. Came back again. When I was about eight she spent two or three summers up at a place called Proctor. It's near Nelson, D .C. where her brother was on the boats. He was a widower by then. And my grandmother kept house for him. My little sister was born off there. That was when I was eight.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it difficult for your mother to be living in a isolated family?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No. She was just. That's what I say. She was another unique person. She never minded it. We're out there. We had a three room house. Very well and very sturdy. But I don't know. Daddy was the original hippie. You know. The bare essentials in life. And plain. The walls were just nice clean first grade lumber but that was it. The ceiling was the same. Table was handmade and underneath here was a nice little shelf. Well just a complete table underneath the table. He made that. He made that. Well he made the whole thing. When you got through eating you put your butter and your bread and all your things underneath here. But mother finally prevailed on them and she got rid of that in Duke time. But boy it was a struggle. He had set up shelving right under the table. Yeah. Just like a whole table top underneath this one. That was his batch variety. It was one little cook stove. Wood cook stove. And sort of a chaise lounge made of leather. You've seen those old fashioned ones. Just a very very few books. History of the World and Modern Eloquence. I can remember them now. I was always dying for books in a roll top desk. Very small place. A spring where you got water. Plenty of wood. A swing in a tree in a hammock. And then you could see the town below. And he had it all fenced. Very nicely fenced with these old fashioned wood rail fences all around 168. But he was gone and his mother is so little it's lovely. She says, Mommy how lucky I am up here. I can read. And she did. Of course she could borrow novels. They weren't using novels. They were pretty good in history and poetry. She liked. She read very good books. And she wrote. She was just writing constantly. There was letters flowing back from her and her five sisters. Her mother. And then there was a few people would come out. It was quite a little hype. During the summer we had quite a few people. And we walked down. We tried to get out on Sundays. Go to church. And then maybe once during the week to pick up our mail. And then fall my father would come. But she had never seen father. And all he asked the daily thing was to go and get milk about half a mile away. And I was about seven or eight. I was able to go alone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was your father gone so much?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well as it were you see. He was out taking care of these mining claims. Absolutely isolated place in Idaho and in British Columbia.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Taking care of them for himself or someone else?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: His claims. And you had to do so much during the summer. Then he had a beautiful place out on the west coast of Prince Edward Island. Vancouver Island. It was a, I think there was about a hundred, maybe two hundred acres. And there was about a hundred acres of a dike. He worked real hard. He and another German man. The dike. They both had property together in this particular area. That's towards the north end of Vancouver Island near Quatsino Sound. I suppose it would be a, I've never even seen the place. But he'd work on it a little bit. And then there'd be odd jobs. He'd go, I suppose, and help other people perhaps out with his claims. He kept busy all summer long. And when the winter snow come he'd come home and spend, he'd be there until the snow broke. And of course he'd go to town every day or two and get up wood. He and my mother just talked constantly. Talked. That's why I just brought up some conversation. He was just fascinating. His mother was excellent. And they'd debate about things. And women's suffrage was something that was going on then. And I listened to all that. And politics. Just bubbly. And life was very, there was always life in that place. Just life and conversation. Good conversation.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did your mother feel about women's suffrage and how did you bother feeling about it?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, daddy sort of frowned on it. Women didn't know when they were well off, I guess. But mother, she was sport but not belligerent. She always felt she could get anything she wanted with a little diplomacy. I mean, she was an adored wife. So, you know, you can't fight it too hard when you got everything going your way. Of course, he was a little, you know, positive. He wouldn't have fought it too hard. But I mean, he just figured women didn't know when they had it good. But nevertheless, he had very high standards for himself and for the requirements of a husband. I know he'd say, oh, he had too much use for Englishmen. That guy, he never gets the wood in. He won't take a pail of water in. His wife has to milk the cows. And, you know, he... And that's what I always noticed, too. Only the neighbors that I had there in Berlin. But, my gosh, they just won't, they expect the women to do everything. There's some nationalities like that. And daddy all. And boy, you know, you just were brought up. From the time I was just like this, there was a lot of, well, we call them bull honks. You know, we were liberated in those days. These Poles, and they were perfectly good people, I guess. Poles. Bohemians, they did the mining. And you knew. Now listen. When you grow up, you're about to be married to any of those bull honks. They're all right for their own people, but they don't make good husbands. They're too domineering. They expect too much of a woman. I was limited in what I was to have for a marriage from the time I was able to talk. You couldn't marry a first, a guy right over from Germany. He was another tyrant. You weren't to marry him. And an Englishman. Well, that just left you with the Scotch, and maybe second or third generation Canadians, and Scandinavians, maybe. They were pretty good workers. He proved to them. But that's the marriage. It was laid right down. You had your choice, and you knew what to look for. And of course, mother, she threw in the religion. You never dared go out with a Catholic boy. That would be awful. I went once, and I felt guilty all the time. I wasn't with him when I was 20 then. But you know, in your own little safe way, you knew your boundaries, and it didn't bother you. There was plenty there. There was lots of men floating around the country. You knew that. There would be plenty to choose from. And even if you were sort of limited, I never fought it. But my mother and my sisters, and her sisters, they were much more vocal about women's suffrage. And they really were for it. Because it was so unjust. You know all the arguments for it. You couldn't have property in your own name. At that time, were they very... Were they realized just all these... Yes, they did. They knew it then. And they really howled about it. Because they realized that if you had a good husband, and you know, sort of a benevolent dictator, you were fine. But you could see that other women... They didn't have a good husband. They didn't have it. And he'd take the children, and he could be untrue to you. And you didn't. You just had to put up with it in order to keep your children. That was the problem. That was one of the things. You know divorce is almost unknown up there too. I was brought up there in Wainwright where we were married. There wasn't a divorced woman there. A mother never had a divorced woman amongst all her queens. They didn't like their husband to kill them. Well that's what the Ukrainians did. They had a deep way. But the Bingley's did. They put up with it. One woman, she got so sick and tired of it. I don't blame her. She was a Ukrainian. Yeah. And you can always see... Didn't know what to do. It mattered well about 85 feet deep. And she threw a stick down there. She says, you know, there's a big stick down there. And I said, well, let me get it out. And of course she didn't get it out. So he went down and she pulled the ladder out. Or the rope, whatever it was. So he couldn't get out. She went the hardest up the horses. You know, she had to do a lot of that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You like coffee?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh yes. And she loaded up a big load of rocks. And she just kept duffing them in. It didn't matter how he howled. She just kept duffing them in. Until everything was quite nice. I'll tell you, that's the way she got rid of them. Well, they thought they ought to have a little fork, you know, but nobody would stick up for him. Well he was brutal. One of our neighbors knew him. He couldn't get drunk and spend all the darn money and come home and beat up on her. That's the way he'd do it. Our neighbor knew him. He was a Ukrainian. He said, oh, he needed killing. But it was in the paper. This was one of these big crimes up there. That's the way some of those guys were.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think he would beat her up pretty regularly?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, every time he got drunk. Every time he got money so he could go in and get drunk. But evidently she'd protest without anything and beat her up. You know, naturally she'd go. You could understand why my dad put down the, he knew it. Well that may have been one out of, maybe there was only one out of ten that was like that. But now my Ukrainian neighbor's sister, he was like that, Mrs. Kadoras' sister. They had nothing in the house. They were poor. There was six or eight children. There was one coming along all the time. They slept on the floor. Old, big rags of quilts on top of straw. And no bed. There was never a bed in the house. No bed's bed. And finally, one time they did have, they got a little bit of a crump.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he do? Did he buy blankets for the kids or her?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, he went and got himself a fur coat. You know, it was that kind of thing. The boy was sixteen. Alrighty, goes out to work, harvesting, made a little money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he do with his money?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: He comes home by himself to do the buns of cat logs, a lovely new bed. Sleeps on it himself with his mother on the floor. That's some of the things. People are right from Europe. They're raw. They're untamed. They don't even speak your language. They're fine. Get them another generation and they're tamed down. But they need some of those rough edges taken off them. Oh, this Godot that told me how to mix mud and everything. He was a very nice guy. And he used to tell me about all these Ukrainians around there. And what happened to them. And how they treated their wives. I told him, I said, my God, you'd better be good or your wife will treat you that way. But he never had any trouble. Well, he doesn't like them. That's why we moved down. He was the only one that moved into our district in Waini. Now, this is instant hot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you like something in it?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, just black. I mean, we've got milk. So, this woman that killed her husband in the well, you said it was a trial, but she wasn't found guilty.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have a trial?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, yes, they did. They had a trial. Everybody stepped up for her. They all stepped up for her. And she may have gone to prison for a while, but she wasn't killed. They might have put her in overnight, you know. But we got started, I guess, saying that there's, divorce is so hard to get. There's only one ground in Midelepe. And it's not always easy to prove. A wife had to prove it. She has to prove it. You bet you have to get a lawyer. If you're rich enough, you can, they'll take, I mean, the man will, you go to a lawyer. Okay, well, I know a woman, so and so, for $50, she'll meet you at a hotel. All you have to do is register as a man and wife and go up to a bedroom. My detectives will break you in a couple, maybe a hundred, a hundred and fifty, that was before the conflation's wake. And they'll go in there and they'll see us in the bedroom. You know, as you're doing anything, just sit in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What if the husband wanted to get rid of his wife? Was it easier for him?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, not even here. He'd have to fix the same thing. But if he wanted to, now this is how they'll do it. If a husband wanted to get rid of his wife, and he could come to some agreement with her, he'd take the rap. Even if he wasn't guilty, he'd go. He'd be the man, that's all. He'd go to the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did this make for a lot of unhappy marriages up there?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, it didn't. It didn't. You, I don't, I think they were happier than here, because you knew it was going to last. You didn't fuck around. No. And there were some common law marriages.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Why they'd come to the conclusion, I'm never going to get my wife, and yet they were still legally married, and there'd be somebody that they'd maybe live together. There was quite a few common law marriages like that. But divorce, it wasn't a divorce person in our district. My mother, as I say, I never, but then, the next generation's different.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do I need milk or sugar?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, that's fine. We're going to have dinner. I've got dinner coming up. Now, my sister was the first one in the family to get a divorce. She had an alcoholic husband, and it came to us.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And he was a man of principle, he thought. I mean, as far as women was concerned, only he didn't drink. And they were separated for several years before the family came down to it and gave her the evidence. It was all arranged, but... But of course there are, there were lots of cases where... but it's a genteel thing to do. Yes, I don't feel good. But, we kind of got off our track, I was going to... maybe I'll have a talk to you. I never had time to shave this morning. But he had time to eat. But I didn't feel like it. You didn't feel like it. No, sir, I just didn't... it was the time, I suppose. I had to shave about twice a week. Well, there's... but you know... now I noticed the difference when I came here. There's more freedom when the marriage to me is firm, or that you know there isn't going to be a divorce, the thing isn't going to break up. You have... you go to these dances, you go to these little parties, you're much freer with a man. I mean, you talk. And your... your friendliness isn't taken as a come on. Because he knows there's a boundary, there's a stop off right there. This is where you don't go any further. I'm married, you're married, and it's friendly here, but that's the end of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was different up there, as you remember?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, much different than down here. Down here where divorce is so easy. You start a friendship with a man, well, I mean, it can go on and on. There, there was just such a... there was such a boundary, there was just... there was barriers, and you knew it. And he knew it, and...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that huge?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: You could be more open. Here, if you saw... you know, I didn't notice here, especially amongst these things. You could see her, she was talking to him. Not that they said it about me, but you couldn't have... I don't think there's as much freedom.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that's because of the times changing as time went on, or just between American and Canadian?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: That was American and Canadian. I think that was... I think it was because of the divorce, the easiness of divorce down here. You're all... if you're... say a person's been married and divorced several times, well, it's sort of a... it's become a casual thing, or...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know people down there get divorced more than down there?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, no, but yes. Oh, yes. We always knew it. We always knew it can have a comment on it. It is... you know, my mother came from the only way you could get a divorce was an act of paralellment. You had to go through the... well, just like you'd have to go down here to Boise to get a divorce. But now it's changed. There are other things. I think you can get it for a man's insane, or a wife's insane. Well, you know, they must have had a strong bond with your parents, because it seems to me that six months of separation would be rough on a marriage. I mean, today it would be... it would almost kill a marriage, but back then I would think... Look at these seafaring people. I don't know whether it was hard work, or maybe they drank more. I don't know. I just... I mean, the men are just what it was, but my mother was quite happy. She really was. She never needed for anything, and she was a person that could adapt to almost any condition, seemed to me, and make a goal. But now, everything slid along pretty good until World War I came along. I was nine. I just turned nine. And Daddy wasn't feeling so good, that's the point. See, he was about twelve, fifteen years older than Mother. And he put off going out. August the war came on. Now, I'll tell you about war. Of course, I'd read stories. Remember, I told you I'd learned to read. That was so exciting. And I'd read about wars, and the thrill of battles. And I asked my mother, would the men come through the path, this crow's nest path on horseback. I could see them, black horses, white horses, and I was out in my swing thinking I was scared. And here it is over in Europe. Oh, she says it'd be a long, wild year before they get here. Let's not worry about it. But I was still not too easy about it. Then September, Daddy was going downhill. Now then, the mines, it threw everything in an economic slump. As far as our work and our income was concerned, everything just froze right up. And my name was Scarce, very scarce that winter. Daddy was up and around always, but what was happening was drops he caused by his heart failing. He carried such huge pecs on his back and worked so hard, that's when he opened all his life. And it was failing. So, the second summer came, you see, then. And he decided to go to this homestead. Well, it was a bop place, it wasn't really a homestead. Out on Vancouver Island, he was going to sell his homestead. We just sell our improvements. The Dykeland. The Dykeland, we were going to go out there. And we finally put the place up that we were on just for improvements. We got $400, can you imagine, is all we got to move on. And our little bit of furniture packed up in August. And in July or August, we started to move out slowly towards Vancouver Island. Mother was serene and happy and she had a black dress, new black dress, high collar, and she looked lovely. She was about 38 then. And we stopped at Nelson's, saw her mother and her brother. And then we went to Vancouver, she had a sister there. You know, families were so important. You just, it's just like jumping from stone to stone. You make your contact before the next step. And they were a very helpful, kindly family. Then we got on the boat. The boat only went twice a month. Went to Victoria, met an old friend of hers there.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: We had to wait, I think, a week for the boat. Then we started up to Vancouver, quit Quatsino Sound. And pleasant weather, landed in Quatsino. Daddy had some old friends there. Then we came, there was no place, we started, we were coming down with what we thought was chicken pox. And the mother was right. We had a sleep in the freight shed of the, where the boat docked. Then in about a week we got groceries together, hired a boat, and went 25 miles, not to the Dykeland, but to an abandoned fishing camry. Oh, I think it was 8 or 10 miles away. So, this little open boat, just a little bit of a thing, not much bigger than here to here. I think you must have taken, made two trips and brought more furniture and stuff the next time. Went into this quite a sturdy old house, but nobody lived in it for 8 or 10 years. There was a stove. And we were, oh we had hooping cough to a fair u -l all September. My little sister and I know a doctor, but there was an Indian village. About a mile away, just across this little inlet. And they were really nice old time, untouched, primitive Indians. They hadn't been Christianized or anything, hadn't been tampered with. I remember going over there, Daddy was talking to them, and he could still get around. And seeing them come down in the evening, and they were washing their nose and their throats. They'd take a great big swallow or a big mouthful of this good salt water. They'd gargle, and then they'd take another one and they'd go whoosh through their nose. And then sometimes it would go up this way and down. I saw these six race men doing that. Well, Mother's always told me it was good to gargle the salt water. I was 11 then. My little sister was kind of a little younger. Well, she was just 3 years old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did the family make this move? Why did your father?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Because he thought, you see, up there in the rock he said it was very high. And he thought if he could get down to sea level he'd feel better. You know, Mother went along, she didn't argue, well, Daddy if you think you could feel better I'll go along. Everybody thought she was crazy. Well, I think he was right. I don't know dear. It was easier. It did, it was natural. But it didn't cure him. But there's no doctor at that time. No, it didn't cure him. He was working too hard. He'd have to quit hard work. I know, but he had quit. He hadn't done a thing for a year. It was too far for anything. But she just went along placidly. She was the most mid -Victorian submissive wife you ever saw. She had no arguments. Just made out. And here was a dying man on her hand, 25 miles from civilization. Oh, maybe 75 miles from the nearest doctor. We did have a little medication we brought up from Victoria. She was naturally held there. She knew how to take care of her. The Indians were superstitious. I remember you could see their burial platforms. They were burying their dead. Way off you could see their burial platforms. And they had these canoes, dug out canoes, you know, with the high piles. And there was one or two fly heads. Still, you know, the board. There's one woman with her head been on. She had a board. They used to put boards here and here. That's one of their babies.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they quietly living?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah, I think one of them. They put that on their babies. So the head will stay that way. And they had iron, some of them had iron bands around their legs. They just put on their, well, I guess they were adults, young adults. You could see the flesh on top and bottoms kind of growing over it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have much to do with that?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: They'd come over, but they were, but, now that was September. It was quite a brief time. They'd come over a time or two, looking mostly for whiskey. We did have some. Daddy would give them a little shot of peace, just hand it out to them. He had a few bottles for himself, you know, just in case of sickness. And he tried to get them to, we would pay them five dollars if they would bring our meals from this place that was 25 miles away. Well, they did once or twice. And then they brought us medicine once. We finally got some more medicine for him. Then he was just going down. He thought, oh, that was another incident. Mother had rode a boat as a girl. I had never learned to row, although I had been around the water at the Kootenai Lakes, but never had a boat. But there was an old boat, and Daddy told us how to cock it, you know, put some rags around and make it seaworthy. And we found four oars, and I'd practiced and Mother had practiced, and we finally went over to the Indians one time and got that far and come back again. I was a pretty good size for 11. And this time we went over at night in the evening, and they turned us down. They wouldn't take Daddy, and Mother realized that he just had to get to a doctor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They wouldn't take him? What would you do?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: They were scared. They were afraid of the superstitious of a sick man like Ken.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But what did you want them to do?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: We wanted them to take him, well, even to a cannery that was five miles away, another cannery, and have them take him to Quatsino. And maybe there he could get on a boat, this twice a month boat, and go down to Victoria. She was just desperate. She sent messages up to see if they should get help. That's what you wanted them to do. And they refused. They didn't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what did we do?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: It was a calm day. Calm morning, early in the morning. We thought we'd try them once more, maybe offer them more money. We didn't even, we just had very old clothes. I remember Mother had a pair of gum boots that Daddy's on, and we weren't dressed up special, just a gold cross there. And asked again, and we did pretty good rowing, and they turned us down. Mother says, I think we'll go to the cannery. And you have, I'll show you the map. You have no idea what that involved. It wasn't so far, but it was the open water. You come out of this inlet. We were here, the Indians were here. And then you swung around close to, well, the lighthouse was out there at a distance, but it was shipwrecks and everything else that occurred there. Then you went up another inlet for several months. We had to cross the open water. Open water is nothing between you and Japan. And it was calm, and Daddy was wise about tides, and we hit the tides right. But just about the last mile, the tide was turning, and oh, we had to work. Daddy was, he was able to bail. And we finally got to the cannery. And I know my mother, she told the cannery owner what had happened. And he thought he was the bravest woman he'd ever seen to take a trip like that. I don't know, it's one of those things you never forget. So he got his lunch, little boat, all little open lunches. Took her over to our friend, the Leesons. But here we were, still lingering on with the whooping cough. He left us off at a, well, everybody was friendly, a young couple. And they took care of us at this limestone island a couple of miles away from Mother. While Mother made arrangements, the boat had just gone. And finally there was a man by the name of Peterson. Naturally he was a Swede or a Norwegian. A little open boat. And he agreed to take him to the Indian hospital. That was clear around the north end of Bancu Island. It was just incredible, some of those things they did then. And he was just a marvelous navigator. And that meant open water all around until he hit the inside. And he took him to Alert Bay. It was an Anglican mission hospital. And that left Mother and I on this side. And he stayed there a month. Well, Mother and I then went back again, I guess for a while. Just previous, I guess we, let me see, I guess he was gone. And we went back there. And by that time, who should come along but the old German with eight kids. Mother was going to teach school. And the government already got in action. They had sent in supplies to build a bore for us. Here we were ready to leave. Of course, Daddy, oh he was going to get well and come back. Well they built this and this old German was there in hell. I call him old because he had a beard.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he looked, what was his name?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Right out there in this dike land. Because he owned the dike land next to Daddy and they were going to start a community. But he came out there to kind of help build on this war. That would maybe be their, that was an easier place for a boat to come. So they built this little war. Mother, then Mother was getting around November and the end of October close to November. And she decided she'd have to leave for the winter. Always just leave for the winter. She took her things back and stored the little things we had. I remember we gave, that's Mr. Peterson's, my dad's gun. I guess maybe he didn't do it until after he died. But we come back and I remember sleeping. We couldn't go in. I don't know, we were such healthy kids. But nevertheless we never could go in this woman's house because she had a child that was quite delicate. And the children died of whooping cough then. It was only a year and a half old. And my little sister and I had a sleeping up and a partly built launch. That this man was building, I suppose it would be as far as from here to that spring. No not the German. This was when we went back to Quatsino afterwards. No we had a little house when the German was, when we were with the German. No you see, the thing got built, the German departed. The mother decided she couldn't live alone there. And so she decided she'd go back to Quatsino Sound and then eventually go down to Victoria and Vancouver, which she did. But you're waiting, you see you're always waiting a couple of weeks for the boat. We always missed the boat. And daddy was gone to the hospital, clear on the other side of the island.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And when we got back we had to wait again. My sister and I had a sleep in this launch right beside the inlet, the ocean. It was very pretty, kind of cold though. We were in the little lantern. I remember reading books in the lantern trying to use my little sister but couldn't go in any house. Oh it was kind of rough. Mother was beautiful, serene, calm. Everything was going to be alright. We'd come back next spring. We'll go down there for winter to Vancouver and I remember her brother sent her $50 to help out. So that's what we did. We left her stuff, took our personal things and went to Vancouver, Victoria, then Vancouver when the boat came in. And my mother didn't know it but in the meantime my father was getting so bad that they shipped him down with a different boat on the other side of this big Vancouver island to the Vancouver General Hospital. So my sister and I stayed there in a hotel in Vancouver with mother for about a week. And we saw our dad for the last time in the hospital. It was Vander who was close to Port George, Prince George. It was right in the interior. It was two nights, two days I think on the boat and a day and a night on the train. And I went to see my little sister. And the trauma of leaving mother, you know, but nevertheless no tears, nobody cried. You never thought of crying in those days. Everybody had too much work to do to cry. You may cry later on but not then. Nobody had no tears at any of these times. No tantrums, no fanatics. So we're going up to see Aunt Amy. I liked her. So one thing, there was no welfare, nothing like that but you had a good rapport with all the members of the family. And you knew Aunt Amy loved you. She had no children on her throne. You knew you were going to be taken care of. There was that. There was security. You weren't just tossed aside like into some of these states here. There was love. You never felt lost. Something happened to your mother. There was all these aunts and uncles that had always been kind to you. Maybe they weren't just quite as good but it was alright. They were pretty good. So we went on our little trip and I didn't feel too good I guess. I wasn't too seasick but it was alright. Second boat trip around. Made our changes on trains and 4 o 'clock in the morning. We got off at Vanderhoof. November cold and clear. Nobody there but the town drunk and the station man. Nobody. Poor little me and my little sister and all those horrible things we hear. You hear them even when you're young. The town drunk was friendly and he says let me carry her. The little sister. I says no I'll carry her. Of course she was lumped. Little miss lumped lumped. Three years old. Dead as day. I struggled with her. The station master struggled with our two suitcases. We went to a hotel or boarding house. There was a very bosom -y woman came. I don't know why. Children knew. I knew I was very worldly wise. I didn't know the bloody details but I knew there was women and women. She looked like a madam to me. I was only 11. I don't think she was but she looked like a madam. I said I've been around so we'll say. I said boy I don't know. She's maybe alright but. So she put me upstairs in a nice bedroom with a huge I think they call them those wardrobes. That was a thing of horror. Peeked under the bed. My little lamp. I sat there with the lamp going. No aunty. Nobody.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who's going to take care of me?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: So I guess about 5 .30. Aunty came and her husband. He'd been out on a trapline. Never got home until 12. There was telegraphs. She'd had the message but she lived 12 miles out there with a horse and wagon out in the country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So she didn't know that you were coming?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: She knew we were coming but there was no way to get there until her husband came home. He never got home until 12 off a trapline. Oh boy was I glad to see her. I thought that was about a goner in that hotel. Never been on those traplines.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You've never been around one of those traplines?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No never on a trapline. Well we had one. Carl and I when I first went up there the first year. He said well we'll trap this winter. And I know when we started out it was 56 below. I thought my it's cold to go making that coops.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you call it? Well coops yeah well what's coops?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well I learned. You take a log that's laying this way and another one that way. You put sticks around and put your bait down a certain way and then use sticks around you know in the snow. And have it so that and then you pack the trail down. You see when you're going around your trapline you make a trail. Carl would take a step. He's taller and heavier than I was. He'd make a step and I'd step right in between his steps. And every time we went around we made the trail a little better. Then from this trail to the coops about 50 feet. Well I always figured about 50 feet. We'd track back and forth. I would go back and forth while he was fixing them you know. And get a real good trail so they'd follow this. They'd follow a man's trail. And they'd get on that and they'd go right in. They'd smell this thing. And here's a little thing. You got this snare just the side of my hand like that see. You got two sticks stuck down here. And you got a stick about this long hanging up here with a snare. I think it's four or five little copper wires twisted you know. And it's just long enough that it'll make that loop around like this and back in here. And they're fairly close to the stick. So when he comes through here we have a blade of grass tied around this stick here and around this one. And this mustn't be too big now. Remember it's only that big. See and he comes through here the first one and he's got to break that blade of grass. See and when he breaks that it tightens right up around here and it keeps going in. And he pulls that stick off the top. But he's got the smell of that and it's out just about that far. And once he smells it and figures well I got to get rid of this stick. He backs up and he puts one foot here and you know that thing flops around. He puts the other one up there. And he just lays down like that until he dies. He'll catch that end that's flopping around. He'll catch it when he gets that hold. He never let go. Just choked himself to death. The furthest we ever found one was all about 30 feet. He got one too short or something and he just kept rolling and rolling. And you could see it in the snow. A little skip to snow you know it had been moved like that see. Followed that and found it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would you trap there mostly? What was it that you were trapped in?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Link. It was Link.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This night it was 56 below you went out?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: 56 and we had to go out and make these coops. Now you know what those coops are. If your father -in -law was laying like that he'd have an easy time making them. He'd make one and just live a while.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: We had 50, I think we must have had about 50 coops around there. Anyway. But we had to go around that's 12 miles. And we got that made in a day or two at 12 miles. And then after that we had to go whether we got anything or not. You had to go around and tramp that trail. Go around and go into the cooping back and make that good. Well one night we went into the Moorweaver's settlement you know. Where Caroline knows it was a settlement. And go down there and they wouldn't let us go back for two or three days. You'd take in the dances. Because they used to come up there and stay all night and hunt.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And Caroline?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah. So when we'd go down there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your place you mean?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah well they'd come up and hunt.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where were your places?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well this wasn't my place this was my partner's place Carl Logan. And so they'd just come up there and stay all night and we were great friends. With anybody. Anybody come we'd always ask them to stay overnight. And so we'd go hunting the next day of course that's what they come for. But anyway we got around this trapline. Got that all fixed. Well we got quite a few that winter. Not too many. But we got around twelve dollars apiece for them. It kept us going you know. And it was kind of exciting you know I was young then. Twenty something. And fifty six below I wonder. Yeah and my dad says why cattle couldn't live at fifty below you know. And he said you better stay all winter. And I liked it you know when I got up in Alberta I loved it. That prairie country and things I just thought was wonderful. And so I stayed all the time. I got used to it just like anybody else. Only I had to dress different. Instead of having these ordinary clothes we have down here I'd get heavier underwear.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said to some of those guys what kind of underwear are you in?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: He says Fred Stanfield. Well of course I didn't know what that meant. And so well Stanfield underwear was a good place to buy. It was an extra good see. That's underwear. They had different kinds. Different labels. They start out with maybe the green, the black. Black is the heaviest. And then the red. And then the yellow next. And then the green. And I don't know there's some other color. And one thing about us long staple. Never itches you. Long staple wool. And never itches you like what we had down in the United States. Ours was short wool and I couldn't stand wool I didn't think. But when that cold weather was like fifty six below. Common and just little stove like this or smaller than this to heat the house. Well you had to have something pretty warm. And it was a log house and it wasn't even chinked I don't think. So we got tough. Or I thought I was beginning to get tough. The other guy was used to it. And he told me he was from Michigan.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you know where I found him?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: When the war came up he'd have to go or he'd be conscripted. And he wanted to volunteer. He never said a word to me or any of his friends or anybody out there. He just was messy. And the next thing I knew he was here in Portland. I went into Portland. My brother, one of my brothers died there in Portland. And his daughter saw it. And she said Steiner, Ring Siege, or Seinman Ring Siege died. A hundred feet in relation to Steiner. And so they looked up the phone. Of course I had the phone. And Seinman had the phone. But they called his wife. My brother's wife. She still lives there. And so she said well yeah he lives over there in such a place. And so they called me up. And I couldn't believe that it was Bokeh. She said, well no but I couldn't believe that he lived there. Well any place around. He was from Bokeh, Michigan or someplace. Anyway he told me the town that he came from. You said Vincent you had seen him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that a 17?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: You'd seen him and this was about...no this was later than that. This was after you'd retired. This was about oh I'd say about 58.

speaker: Sam Schrager
Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I know it's an awful long time. A long time. Well he wasn't married. No he had a daughter. Well taller than her and she was 20 something. She had a little girl. And she...so I heard this woman talking.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She said did you know Carl Bokeh? No she said...how was she started that now? She said did you know Carl Bokeh in Alberta?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well I said yeah I knew Carl Bokeh but not down here. I didn't know anybody down here for that name. I said he'd go east if you went any place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And well she said from Caroline?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: She said yes. Well I said sure I know him. Well then Carl was living there at the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Grandson? Hey?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oregon City. Oregon City. Just south of Portland. Well I said we'll have to get together. So the only thing was just before I met him now he was crippled up. He'd been in the sawn in the blue mountains. You know crossed with a chainsaw. And he said he just had 15 dozen trees to sow. And the guy told him oh he said forget them. Just leave it. No he says I'm going to saw them. So he went ahead and saw them and somebody else was working around there and a tree fell on him. And it left him all crippled up so. I guess he had diabetes and that's why he was quitting. But he had to do that and that really put him on the balance. Well I wanted to ask you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you grew up down the central ridge did you have a desire to go up to Canada at that time?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well yes. Yeah. Sure. You know I come up here from southern Idaho. I was down there three years. And I come up here and visit with the folks. I poached. And I said hey I think I'll go up to Canada. You know they had war up there. And in 16. And we didn't have war down here. And I thought well I'll go up there and join the army and go down to France. Have a nice little trip. See. So I said you don't have to be afraid of anything like that. I said you can get killed. You can get killed going down to the barn or some place like that. I said nothing can be traded. So I went up there and then I thought after a while. You know I wouldn't go to volunteer right away. After a while I thought I'd join up. Something happened. This happened. Somebody was getting five dollars for joining guys up. Well I wouldn't stand for that. I wanted to go in and join myself. And I couldn't get anybody that would go in with me. You know. Just like I did just for fun. You know. But they went no. Nobody wanted to go. So finally they told me. Well the Canadians told me. They said you're crazy. We're all trying to get out. See. We're all trying to get out. And I said well what's the matter. See I couldn't understand. Oh yeah they were jumping out of the trains and everything. Getting out and then here I'm trying to get in. Well listen I'm not doing it in a favour. I said I better keep out of this darn thing. Wasn't as much fun as you thought it was going to be. Well I wasn't doing the Canadians any good. Looks like. Me jumping in there. They was jumping out of the train and me jumping in to get in. That didn't look very good I didn't think so I stayed out. And I never did go in there. They couldn't draft me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the war kind of unpopular among the Canadians?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: You know I hope it's there. And all that but you know I was too old. They wouldn't have me. And I wanted, well when the states got in more. See I wrote down here for a questionnaire so I could fill it. You know for, so they could draw me if they wanted me see. I thought that'd be the best. So just about the time the thing was over they sent me a questionnaire.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I filled it out and sent it in myself and I was supposed to take it in to a, to a, to a, what do they call them?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Notary public. Notary public you know. And I just marked it A1, everything A1. Right on down. And I might not pass I don't know. The only thing I thought that might turn me down was my feet. But I was good every other way. I was the one who could safely turn me down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well Stier how did you make a living when you went out there? What did you get yourself down?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: When I went out there I could get a job any place. They wanted me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I went to Calgary and I said have you got any jobs on hand?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah sure. I was making them. They could do anything but you take a Canadian or a special agent he couldn't get a job. They wouldn't give it to them. They had a hundred of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How come?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Because they wanted them to fight. As long as they had a war they wanted them to fight the war.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: See?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And so I get a job any place. And I know a fellow says, yeah I got a job for you. He says a fellow wants a machinist.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What the heck is a machinist?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh he says nothing to it. He says they pay a little more money for that. He says you can fix a disc or anything like that. Oh yeah I could do that. Sure. I knew all about farm machinery and everything like that. He sent me up there and this fellow was from Boston, United States. And he was hiring a lot of guys and he was mean to these Canadians. By golly I said I don't, I watched that he treated everybody like human beings.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And so I got to work in there and he said you're the, I was his, what do they call those?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: He was a machinist anyway. And I thought well what thanks you got to do. Well he says you know, or the two men, two men, a big Swede and a big Norwegian, I think they were close to seven feet tall, weigh about 200 pounds a piece you know. Not any fat on them but they were big and strong as any mules you ever had. And they got a mill in there, let's see, a fanning mill. You know they used a fanning mill up there a lot for fanning the ice out of the grain. You know thrashed it late or winter maybe and a lot of the ice went into the grain and they fanned it out and they used it for a lot of things. Fanning the weeds out and things. And this fanning mill was big as a small thrasher machine you see. And they had a great big building there like a warehouse you see. Nice place to work. This farmer and so he says to me, or these two older guys, these boys, they've been working there for 14 years. Ever since they came in there. And they said, they put this, when this thing came up there, there was what they called an A -frame. Like this. It would come down there and set it on the floor and they didn't know what to do. I guess they couldn't read maybe. And there was a thing to read and tell you every part. Well it was just hard to do it. And I knew what this, I could take a binder, buy a knockdown binder in this country. It gets knocked down, it's a lot cheaper. It gets out here and there's a piece of paper that tells you every part to put together. And I could do that and I said that's what this is. So I looked and saw this piece of paper telling me all what to do. I said yeah sure. Well these two fellas said you think you can put it together. I said I think so. But I said I gotta have help. You know I won't be alone. So I said I want Charlie, another boy about my size. Young fella about 17. And even if we didn't do anything like that, that's alright, get some help. So I got him and we fooled around. Of course I'd read this and I'd be careful. The next thing had to be done. Maybe you say what the heck, you want to put that in and say this goes in next. Well you have to figure that out because nobody else knows. And I know there was one thing before I put this other frame with whatever to it. This nail boat had to go in. I said to heck with it, I can't figure it out right now. I'll put the other thing first and if it has to go in I'll take this thing out and then put it in. Sure enough I hadn't done it. But anyway we had to put it all together that one day. And it was a good size, it was probably a good size engine you know. And a belt, they had a belt of 5 -6 inches. This was the fanning belt. Yeah, oh it looked like a little thrash machine you know. Good size. And so, and this place was a place they told me, they had three crews. One on the job, one to go and one to come. It was a hard place to work. And the main thing is he got an Englishman, he got that fire. You know he was on to that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well that's order I guess he got. But when it comes to America, that's the only people they could work. See, couldn't do nothing with it. So I could ball him out or anything. And whenever I saw him picking on those Canadians, boy I balled him out good and plenty. I really ball him out sometimes. Well anyway, I got this thing all up a day and a half. We had it up, well we was tired of sitting around watching it. Screens and everything all fixed up. Then they had to get the, there was a lot of things I showed them out. They didn't know I could do anything. And they was out there sowing sacks. You know they was going to haul off a bunch of this pelt. It's a wheat. It looks like barley, but it's a wheat. And they were sowing, they were going to ship three carloads. And sowing it. When I looked at it, I said, you got it. They wanted me to sow some sacks. I said, you haven't got a sack needle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well what's that?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I said, that's a patching needle. You know to patch sacks with it has a curl like that, see. You know to patch the sacks with.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well what do they look like?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well I told them it's a straight needle about five inches long. And it's wide. It's wide for one man. And I said, you put that, you could sow the sacks for that. Well he says, I'll get you one. And it was about five miles out of Brooks, Elbert. He got in and he had a little buggy, a light buggy and shafts with a little black horse. Dobbins they called him. And my gosh that horse, you know, he managed to touch the lines the way he went. He went out there and back in just a little while. It wasn't over an hour I guess. And I looked. Yeah, he had a sack needle. Well now he can sow sacks. So now they've been using a patching needle. No wonder it was slow, see. Now I says, I've got to have a sack needle, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well what's it just saying?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well I said, I'll show you. So I throw this one out, you know, and cut this needle. You cut the thread with it, see, stick it on the side. Put it in the bunch here and stick it in the side like that and be ready for another. And I take the sack by the ear and flip it over on my lap. Get up and you get rid of it. Either throw it in the wagon or throw it in the pile. Well I said, now when I fill this next one up, get up. I said, I want to jig it. I want them to fill them, see. And they tried every way. Some people even tried to put sticks in, you know. And I said, you don't need that. I can bust your darn sacks. Just jigging it. And you know, it's easy. You take a sack, a weight. Their oats up there is heavier than ours. We get sacks if they don't weigh 46 pounds, I think it is. You know, ours used to weigh 32. And if theirs don't weigh 46 pounds, it isn't good oat. And then you put them in the regular oat sack, you know, they get to be 153 pounds, see. And I told the child, I always had charted that young fellow to help me. He was my size and everything. And I said, now when that hits the floor, see, it's going to rebound. And you pull up and I said, it's just as easy as if it's an empty position. And a lot of people think it's heavy. Well, if you let it set for a while and then lift it, that's a heavy load. But if you get it just the right time, I said, it's easy. And so Charlie and I get on that right away, see. And now on that we sew them. They had an old wagon, oh, most un -handy thing. You had to put a square box on it. And you had to throw it about pretty high to the ceiling, to the top of the boards around it. You had to throw it in there. See, yeah, it was a little higher than I could reach. See, and I said, now listen, when we get up here, see, so we'll say one, two. And then I said, we'll jump three and go right in. Like that, just stand off a little ways from that. And the first time we did it, we did it, got fine, we did fine. We could do that right along. And I told them, big, sweet, and that big Norwegian, you know, they put it as tall as that to do it that way, you know. Well, they got a hold of that second. One would take it up and the other would bring it back down, see. And then this one would take it up and the other fella would bring it down. I said, listen, you fellas go to bust that thing. Oh, see. They couldn't get together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What does it matter?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I said, listen, the best way you can do it is to take one in each hand and throw it in there like that. I said, not two in one second.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the matter with this place?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: These people couldn't figure out how to do that stuff right. Well, it takes practice to do that together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But I know, have they ever seen how thrashing is done before?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, yeah. They had thrashed it. Yeah, everything was thrashed and handled in sacks. But how looked there. Now, they had a patch needle. Just imagine how they'd go around there. Hadn't they ever seen the... They haven't, actually. They haven't. No doubt they're Englishmen. With a regular sack needle, you know how you shape the sack, bring it this way, and you get it up there and you just come down. You press the needle down like this.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You've seen those needles, haven't you?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: You go down and up. You go down and up from your hand. You see, well, they thought if they got the hook in that needle, pull it down, it'd come up itself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, listen, was farming kind of a new thing in that part of Alberta?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, it was just something like here, only they went at it a little different. They did have a... The farm equipment wasn't so good, I don't think, Snyder. It was so expensive.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, it was expensive, for one thing. There's nothing old up there. Too old. Seems like you bought anything that was high. Binder's $300. And if you couldn't buy a secondhand one, it's always in use. They were farther ahead. I always figured... I always... You had to buy everything. And if you did buy a secondhand one, you paid a big price for it. I know we finally... My uncle and I, we had to buy everything new. But our drill was $323, but we got a good drill. And, you know, you don't get wheat. My gosh, it was like this. 18 cents a bushel. That at one time. They couldn't make anything down here, so they couldn't make anything up there. And you have a shorter season, you've got a lot of things that makes it harder to farm up there. You know, you still have such a narrow margin. No, I don't think... I have the... They were... They have adapted. They've got better weeks. They've figured things out there now. When you went up there, did you... I worked for the other guy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You worked for other guys?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah. For quite a few years. And then in the wintertime, I worked in the mines. Then you worked with... Because it's cold. Then you worked with his uncle from about... I worked in the... Well, after a few years, I went to Bizbug. And he just begged me. Uncle Ed. You know Uncle Ed. And I just begged... He just begged me to stay here, or to... Yeah, to... For a year, he said. Well, I stayed there three years. I thought, well, see, I don't save my money anyway. In the wintertime, I used to mine in some of those mines. And hurry up when I got out. Hurry up into the beer place and have a good glass of beer and play a little pool. And then supper was ready and had supper. And then maybe after that, go up and play poker and the like. I just had a great time. You spent all your money. Well, I never spent any of that. I was ahead all the time. Oh, you were making... I made the price of my clothes all the time. My uncle thought I did, but I sent enough money to pay tax on his place. I'd pay him $750 now and again, you know. That kept the wolves away. Okay, well, so he asked you to stay. Yeah. He begged you to stay. Well, he said he'd just give you anything to have somebody to stay with. Well, he offered me. We made an agreement. We made an agreement that I had to get $30 a month in the wintertime. And I'd be out working in the mines. In the... yeah. So you didn't work in the mines anymore. Oh, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you did?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah, I worked in the mines.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In the wintertime?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: In the wintertime. And worked for him and the rest of you. And then I sent home money. That's the only way he'd get any money to pay. And then he helped him break land and... Well, and then in the spring I was just dying to get back on the farm because I'd rather be on the farm than in the mines.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where'd you send the money home to?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I'm glad. Ed's around Wainwright.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Also, in other words, you were sending money to Ed to help him get by?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah, well, he had a section of land, you know. And we had to clear up some of it. It all brought him. And his four little kids, they're just coming every year and he had a pretty tough... I know when he started out that they had $8 ,000 and he didn't have a cent to spend. And he got about that much out of his father -in -law. And everything went... He thought, you know, after a while they could cross and he'd make some money. There's no such thing as getting money out of it. You spend it once, it's gone.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, I'll tell you, he was traveling, trying to see this stop at this place, see what the land, talk to the farmers, and then another place around like that. And that all caused money. He started from scratch up there. Now one thing, when he started in, farming was right after World War I. Prices were high. He had a wife and four kids. He had to buy equipment. He had a father -in -law that lived with him. And then the prices kept on going down. That was the... It was sliding on down, you see, towards the... But he had spent before that, he had spent most of it. You know, buying things, you know. Buying equipment. You had to buy all the lumber. You were living out more or less prairie. You had to buy lumber. And food was expensive out there. He built a house that got $1200 for lumber. And I got the house in that place. And mortgage. And besides that, he had some lumber for the barn, I imagine. How much...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How badly did he need you to help him, Steyer?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, he had so much work to do. There's an awful lot of work to do, and it's more than one man can do. You got to go to town 14 miles, it takes up practically a day. You get the paper, you get the paper in the little grocery, and you talk to a few of the neighbors and so on and so on, and your day is shot. You picture us, now we never drive fast with the horses. See, the horse and the buggy thing. 14 miles each way. Each way, you see. You don't drive it an hour. No, a day is shot. Your day is shot. They had to haul water. I had to haul water from his. He had to, and when I come there, I haul the water. And Steyer, let me tell you, he's always superman when it comes to work. No wonder he's uncle. No matter what anybody else could do, he could do it faster and quicker and better when it came to shocking, plowing or anything. Well, I used to shock my acre up there. I could make the money at home. And he figured if he could just... And he needed... And it's not as strong physically. Never has been. And then when he got up there, it was the moment of truth. He saw what he got into. It's just like pouring water down the hole sometimes, getting started. I go up there and... Very few people made money. So he figured that Steyer would be the best kind of worker he could get. Oh, absolutely. And he couldn't afford anybody else. And... Steyer was the one, when he went out in the wintertime, he was bringing in cash. And he sent them some home to help out with taxes. And they had a telephone, I guess, and they had a few things. And you'd just be surprised, even in those years, how extensive. And I never used any money when I was there. All I had to do was feed me. And I smoked tobacco. And it was the plainest kind of... Gave me plenty of smoke and tobacco.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, didn't they pay you a wage? They pay you a wage for helping?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: The contract was to pay them, but there never was any. We put it on the books. It was on the books when they went to settle up. I had $1 ,700 coming. He said, I'll give you that place that you're living on now. $1 ,700 and a figure $2 ,800. Oh, yes. Yeah. And he owed $1 ,000 on it. So, and you take the mortgage over there. The other $1 ,000. So, I got that place. And then I homesteaded a quarter next to his. The one I bought from him. But it took me all this time to get that. I knew about it for years. But somebody would go in there and file on it. Just when the other guy would give it up. And then when this fellow wanted to give it up. He'd talk to a friend of his. Listen, you give me $20. And I'll let you have my rights. And I'll leave it, see. Well, I wouldn't know anything about it. So finally I found out it was open, you know. Nobody would do a thing when they got it. They had no money. They just, you know, transferred it. So I got it. When I got it, I said, boy, I'll just break it up right now. So I started in. Plowing it. And I farmed on it for seven years. I got crops off it. Part of it. 16 acres down next to the river there. That I know one year I put it in the wheat. It got 350 bushels off it. I never did figure that would be an acre. But it is quite a lot of wheat. And then I had 14 acres on top there that I had plowed it. And then I had 44 acres on the one I bought.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The land that you bought from Ed, the land that you got from Ed, was that the 160 in the house?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: That was the 160 in the house. I bought it at Holmesons at this 160. Right next to it. Ed had a hundred and sixty. Part of it was the pulley landers going down to the river. The guy that...

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: My uncle for $600. And my uncle left the drift. Never did. Oh, he paid a little interest I guess. The 9 % interest was awfully high in those times. And finally, when I had to take it over, it was a thousand dollars. So you see, they never did pay much on it. Yes, there were several factors. We married in 26th. Alright, 27th is when we were just getting started. And 27th I guess was that year we had a baby. And the town burnt out. That meant your credit. You know, it was hard on everybody.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The town of Wainwright?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
