words: A lot of business places burnt out. You know, every good town has a fire. You should have it. It's traditional and it had it. Then the 28th was that. The beginning of the Depression. Seemed to be a hit there just simultaneous, but down here. We had a few bad years. I mean, it was just downhill all the time. That seemed to be that way everywhere. And then I went back teaching I guess for a year. Trying to get us on our feet. I had the two little children by then and I... I guess that must have been around 30, 31. I went back teaching for a year and I got another hit. You see the bank, they just were... They wanted security for everything. They wanted to take a lien on everything you have. The only thing I could do, I went out and I had my own money. And I got a brood sale. A real high bred brood sale. A certain breed we wanted. Yorkshire. And we got started and there was mine. It was in my name and the only thing that pulled us through. We did very well in pigs. Well before I left, the first thing I did, I went to the bank and settled up for the bank.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well why?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I thought it would give a darn for the rest of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why your name? That way they couldn't take it?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: They couldn't take it. Anything in his name, if he had sold cattle, the bank would be there to grab it. Yeah, another thing there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They had lien on everything, didn't they?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: There's another thing, they had a law up there in Canada. That you were allowed out of the crop so much that nobody could touch it. Yes we should. So much of the grain, any of the grain, they couldn't touch it. But the grain... I could have a bin full of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But do you realize we put in 25 bushels a week?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: There was a 50 or whatever and we got back about half of that. So we weren't doing too good. It didn't matter what rights we had if you don't have the wheat. We had a very poor year that year. It was just a disaster. Ed said to me the first three years that he farmed the place he got nothing in the wheat. That could be. I know. I know. He was about right. And he had to live. He had to live. Had to feed that bunch. Things were never as cheap. Food was much cheaper down here. Much, much cheaper. Well, one thing, I did make something on the pigs. Now, a neighbor over there had 60 head of hogs that he didn't have my kind. And he couldn't sell one of them. And he said, well, I'll kill him for dog food.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: These were the things that you got that they will?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: These were Yorkshire's. They were special bacon. They were red, but just bacon. They're just like racehorses. The Yorkshire up there are different from the Yorkshire we have down here. They're a little more on the lean. Oh, they're beautifully lean. So they were wonderful. I like them. And the fellow told me, he said, buy some of my... They were in pigs. And this good neighbor of mine, he says, I'm buying one. He says, you're buying one. He says, you know, I'll pick a good little one. I think there's only one or two left when I come there to get it. So I got this one anyway. And she had 13 little pigs. He got one. He picked one. He says, this is a good sign. They got lots of teats. They have lots of little pigs. And she had 18.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, when you're up there, just forget money. Just pay what you have to and leave the others if you got it in the bank. It will leave the bank. Forget it. It just stands there. Just forget you got money. And only pay for what you have to. You just have no idea. I know one winter there, we had potatoes. Well, we always had pancakes. We did have flour. And we had potatoes one meal. And the next meal we had carrots. We had to have a good crop of carrots. And we'll put a little cream sauce on. That was just it. No fruit, no nothing. You couldn't raise fruit. My mother, she sent a little money from... And I sent enough money to give the children cod liver oil. And I'd buy a tin of canned tomatoes. And get them to dole out maybe half a cup of tomato juice a piece to keep them going. And she sent enough money to buy underwear for the kids. There was two little ones then. I was better off than my neighbors. I was lending her patches to put on her children's clothes. She had eight children. Across the canyon. Now she had been well educated. She was American. She was a court reporter now so she wasn't any slouch as far as education. And they were so poor they were sitting in the dark. All winter long in Canada, the only light they had was from something like this. They made a few little dip candles from their drippings. Just... Fornjwish. Fornjwish. She didn't have enough money. They didn't have enough money for even patches. She said if I could just give her patches and I could loan her a patch. I didn't loan her. I gave them to her. But food was so stupid.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you were living on carrots and potatoes then?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah. For one living. Well I could live on anything. He could live on anything but I decided to me it was great. I never craved apples or anything like that. Oh I just died for fruit and Ed Swenson did. I thought I can... This tough guy, it doesn't matter. And he could eat that white, I call it wallpaper paste. It's alien. Gravy. Gravy. I never knew about it until I married him. Every morning. If you have gravy you have to have beef. And you start from there. Well that's kind of...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What winter was this? What year was it?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Let me see this. Before Elaine was born. Oh about 33. 32, 33. Just the next winter I guess possibly it was a little bit better. We didn't even know meat. You weren't working at that time. Oh no I just worked the one time. Then the next year I was pregnant with the... See the two older ones were about, I guess, five or six. And then I got pregnant with the last little girl. I didn't go back to teach anymore then. So what hope did you... And she wouldn't let me work in the mines, I forget. Well you could, I don't think you could have worked, Steiner, during the Depression. Nobody could work. There was no work for anybody during the Depression.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why didn't you want to?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, gee, yeah. Well, when we were first married, well I don't know. I thought we were, you know, he's the old American salesman that my mother told me about. Super man, he was going to make a killing farming. So I married a farmer. I hadn't planned on a mining guy. And I knew mining. It's all right. You work maybe six months out of the year. He did pretty good to work what he did. It's nothing, there's nothing sure. And during the Depression you couldn't get work in mines. You know, I never made money up there, shocking or anything. By the day, it was four and a half a day. I said I can beat that by a contract.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said how much an acre?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Thirty cents. Okay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much you got to cut?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Three hundred acres.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, can you cut to twenty acres a day?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: That's what I could shock. Twenty acres. And if I had a partner with me, I had a partner with me from Cami out up here. He said he was the best shocker in Alberta.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said you are? How much can you shock in a day? Can you shock twenty acres of that good weed?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: He said yeah. Okay, I said so can I. So we just go out and get contracts. And I said you pick your side, you know, like this table, the square.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You want to meet me at that corner? Or you want to go this way or you want to go that way?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: You know, sometimes you find a little lighter place. I'd pick my, I'd always be there. Just about that time or a little ahead.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you, did you stay ahead of these people?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, oh well, this way. We'd never get in a hurry in the morning. Let them cut a few times, see. Then we'd start out. They had to have something to start out. We'd work down to this corner here. And then wait a while. Wait a half an hour, see. So we'd make a few rounds. Then we'd take everything to that corner. And then we'd go into the house, eleven o 'clock. See, that's where we would get that. Then we'd, they'd come in at twelve. And at one o 'clock they'd go back to work. Well, after a while we'd go down there and we'd start there. We'd come to here. Sit here, wait. And a half an hour or so and then get over there. And I got it, there wouldn't be much left. When we got over there, we'd be pretty well left. And I know when we went to work, that first place there, there was two brothers. They had 300 acres, all right. Well, I said it'll be a few days. And, oh, his mother was an awful good cook and we had the best board we had, you know. So, uh, the way it was, here's the, in Alberta, it'd be flat up here. And then drop down like, and flat down here like this table. Well, gosh, there's a hundred feet up the top flat there. And so the oldest boy, he hired us. And they said, you go up on top right after dinner. He says, you boys go up on top and, uh, he says, we got about nine acres cut up there. See. And he says, uh, we'd be up there about two o 'clock or something. Two eight foot binders, see. They cut 20 acres apiece a day, see, that's 40 acres. So, they come up there and, gee, this, uh, we were already around, you know, but we were kind of standing there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the young fellas, hey, he said, you fellas going to play around?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, yes, sure. And that's an hour, see. Like, uh, well, you're supposed to put them in a straight row. Well, I told him, you know, you drop them in the straight, we'll put them straight. We'll tie no loose stuff. Well, he said, you don't even have to raise that. Well, I said, we can raise that up, you know. And we did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Loose stuff?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah, if there was any. But he said, we don't have any loose stuff. We have all of it. Putting all of it on a, he says, I don't care what you do with that. But it had to be in straight rows because they have stoop loaders. You know a stoop is a shock. Shock loaders. And they'd take these stoop loaders about six feet wide, I guess. And they'd just grab the shock and throw it up and get us into a wagon. Big hay rack. And, uh, then they'd dump it off down there at the stack or something. I don't know. So you have to, or the machine. So you have to have them straight rows, well. So that was agreement.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then the guy says, how long do you guarantee them to stand?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I said, till we get away from them.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: This young fella, the youngest brother, you know. Oh yes, all kinds of things. Well, he said, you make too much money. Fine. I said, take us back to town. He brought us out here. And I said, all you have to do is take us back to town. This was the younger brother, you know. I said, we come out here to make money. We didn't come out here to board. I said, if we come out here to board, I always want better board than what your mother puts out. That's fine. But I said, we come out here to make money. The older brother, he said, come on, let's board. So they went to work. They got the 300 acres cut. We come in for dinner. We just shot a little in the morning, a little in the afternoon, you know. But we shot trash. And you know, you take the average. I see him shot really, and believe me, they stayed up. They were just beautiful, symmetrical. Something like that. Yeah, that's another thing. No wonder Ed wanted him. He's just a whiz. Fast and quick. A month after I got through with this place, I happened to meet this guy on the street. I think it was, I forget, it wasn't Let's Bridge. It was another town, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they said, hey, you know those shocks you put up first?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I said, yeah. He said, they're standing, still standing. Well, I said, I didn't expect them to fall down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, good bundles? You had all good bundles?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And I said, if you put round shocks, they won't fall down without you got an awful way. But if you put them up right, I says, drop them down round like that, braced just right. I said, you don't act, don't pick up a bundle again and keep on. Pretty soon you got nothing to see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you didn't work too hard and you stayed ahead of them anyway? Hey? You didn't work that hard and you stayed ahead of those guys anyway?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: That's right, I didn't have to work hard. And we worked at the spells. Boy, I tell you, you'd go up there and pick up two guns like that and you'd set them down this stage. He is just exceptional, I mean, tell you. It's easy for him to keep ahead. It's easy for him, but some of these other guys, it would be a good hard day's work. Like when he'd go to fill up that tank, that great big water tank, the whole water.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where? Here?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, this was in Alberta. He'd just pump and pump and pump. Some other poor soul, he'd be just working his guts out. Well, I'll tell you, it's pretty... He's Superman, believe it or not. It's a 350 gallon tank, I told, up there. Two and a half miles to one of the neighbors, we always got our water there. Real nice water. I'd fill it in 20 minutes for the pump. Tie myself and I'd just go just like that. And that water would just stab your stream into that tank.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Listen, I've got to ask you, where do you think your being a hard worker came from?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, I just figured you had to work. You've got to work. Well, good physical health, that's a good start. All those boys just had a helmer and a steiner, had to work when they were younger. Mother died, they had to take in and...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old were you? About 14 and 16?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, I was...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When your dad left to go to school and left you?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I was 11 when mother died. I know, but when your dad went to go to school and left you farming over there on the ridge. Well, I was 17 and 18.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were younger than that, your dad first went to Missouri to take up the chiropractor work?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah, something north, 17, 18, something like that. You were pretty young.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you start working, did your father start working you when you were pretty young?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah, I'll tell you how old we were. If this was separate, this puts me in the mind of it. Just like this, we had a draw like that down there. Well, right down there, it was a little steeper than this. We had a little tree that dad, I guess, thought was dry. And we had to have some wood and he had to go out and get something out. He wasn't at home very much. And he had something under the tree, another log I guess. And then, so then he started the saw down halfway in the cut. And I was coming six, I wasn't quite six. You know how big I was, and I was small to me. And Helmer was coming eight. See, last May I'd be six, and last June he'd be eight. And we started then, and it was every day until we had to go to school. We had to saw wood, see. And he said, that saw, it's eight -tenny now today, it moved just like that. Just a few teeth come out of the log and back in. So you know, it took all afternoon to saw that little log, maybe that big around. Half of it was already cut. Just a few swaths that dad was making. And we saw the rest of it, too, it was all out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your father expect a lot of you working?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, he got us started working instead of playing. That was our job. We got to work and we got exercise. I think he expected, I don't think they questioned it years ago.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Kids just said, well that's it, there's no, there's no even fuss. It was just so impressive, you know, the little work ethic. And if you worked like that after a while, you'd get through, you'd rather work than anything else.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Ed says, what do you want to do? When we went in together, you want to, who's going to go to town to get the groceries and the mail?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I said, you are. I'd rather stay out and dig stumps or anything. I wouldn't take that job driving. I'd rather be working. And I had a couple of good sized horses and I'd pull stumps, poplar stumps, you know. Throw them on with a dirt arm, weigh maybe 100, 150, maybe 200 pounds. I'd pick them up, throw them in a pile. I'd pile and pile of it and burn it down. We'd plow it all down. And I'd rather do that than have Sunday off and just run around.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh yes. I like my horses. I like my horses because, and they knew I had one horse. My uncle used to just hate him. He'd go up there, he was logging the horse, you know. He'd like to break a single tree. He just loved to. I just liked him because I'd put one chain around this stump and one chain around that stump right over there, see. And he'd tighten up and he'd hit that so hard that that stump would almost break off level with the ground, you know. Then come up just like that. And then he'd just take another stump, another step or so. And he'd find out he'd have to pull another one, you know. He'd snap that one out. The other one was taller and all he had to do was hold. He'd just walk up and hold him. This gray, yeah I think he was a little bit lighter, maybe 50 pounds or so. And he knew he just liked the torment that they did. They liked to jerk him right on his rear. He tried to, but we never could. Oh I love that horse. Well, then Ed sold the whole darn bunch of horses to 19 Head. When he left he had to get the money, I guess, to come down there. He left 10 years before we moved down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, and he got $500 for it and I said, Gosh Ed, couldn't you let me the saddle horse and the saddle?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I said, I'm used to that saddle horse and the saddle. Gosh, he said they had to have the saddle horse or they wouldn't take anything. Well, they're hungry to deal with, I know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who did he sell it to?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, some guy up there, oh no, it was a horse buyer. Oh yeah. Horse buyer, no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And when did you decide that you wanted his part?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, I always liked it up there, but I didn't say that I liked it right there too well. He had to take the part in order to get any money. That was just sort of a...

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I... The water that we had, we had plenty of water, but it wasn't any good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For the house?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: For the house, especially. It was already to stock. I even had to, I put a, when Ed left I took a log about 30 feet long and made a trough out of it, you know, and put it down there for the water to run into this log. And he'd run in there in winter and summer and the stock could go out there and drink.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, you and Ed didn't use that water for stock, did you?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, Ed did, no, he never took time to make anything like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But did you water them tall down there, you and Ed?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah, but we had a smaller trough, see. Oh, I see. You didn't have any... I said, well, gee whiz, I said, why not take that big log we had, big, oh what is it, popular, we called it... Cottonwood down here, I guess it would be. Oh, are... And it was a big one, about 30 feet long, I says that... Bongiglielian, they call them. All I had to do was plug the two, the one end, and I had old Brickey, that's his father -in -law, he wanted to stay with me another year. And if he hadn't stayed with me, I'd have gone to the mines. But he stayed with me, so one morning I just got up a little early in the morning and made that trough for breakfast. And then after breakfast, I took the horses and pulled the trough down there about a half a mile down to the spring. Pushed in, never had no trouble, that water just put in there nice all the time. But you couldn't drink it, Ed tried to drink it, I think I... And he was used to the spring water, you know, couldn't stand that. Well, their diet, they were hard up and their diet was so starchy, and his health went down fast. Well, I was used to starch, I think I was raised on something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Whose health went down?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Ed's. Oh, oh, he, yeah, he... He's lived a long time, but he's always had problems with stomach and asthma. He could work, he could work good when it comes to cutting brush and all that, but he couldn't stay with it. I could stay with it day in and day out, didn't matter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were up there, did you look forward to the time when you couldn't work, like say a month in the winter or something like that?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I'd say, you know, I wanted to work so bad when we was, and the mines was on the strike. And around the mine there, there's all that brush and stuff around there. I just wondered if I could go out and see a farmer that owned it, I'd cut an acre or so for nothing. It used to be worth it, see. Thank God, they just have a little of something to do. Oh, see, they've mined, that's a good, yet, five, six, seven, eight kinds of meat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Five, six, seven, what?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: All kinds of meat. Oh, we were trying to do mines. But old work, there's no fun. His idea of a nice occupation on Sunday is to walk around the fence and see if there might be a staple in this fence post. That's his idea. And up there in Canada, summer and winter, winter you have to get your wood out for the next year. You know, to have it drying out. There's something I don't know. You've got some pictures where we had to pile it. Oh, yeah, well. But you said though that like in the evenings you'd like to go down and have some beer, shoot some pool. Oh, that's what's in the mines. That's what's in the mines. Before he was married. Gay age. Well, you had to do something. And so I couldn't go to sleep until I'd 12 o 'clock or so and try to play poker. And if I lost, some days I wouldn't play. I knew that was the day I'd lose. Three days a week I couldn't play.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what's the other game?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I knew that it was wrong to pay those three days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why, you didn't want to lose, you mean?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I wouldn't gain anything. I'd lose everything I had. You were superstitious about it then. Well, I had got to be.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you know which day not to play? How did you know which day not to play?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, I just learned it from my experience.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A feeling?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: These, working in the mines, I was going to ask you, did you work gyppo there in the mines?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah, all gyppo.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you make any money on that?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, I say all gyppo. No, it wasn't all gyppo. There's some places it's all shift work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about you?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: We call it shift work. So much shift, seven and a half.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Seven and a half?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: That's how you paid them, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A day?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes, seven and a half a shift. Eight hour shift, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you make more than that gyppo?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, you couldn't. You couldn't make near that. They couldn't give you enough cars or something. They couldn't possibly do it. They tried it. And they had to give up. When they said, oh, we can give you all the cars you want and all that, all right, bring them on. And we just loaded up the car and all our cars, cars, cars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the matter with you guys?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: You said you'd give us more cars. Oh, boy. They couldn't do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you guys have to go on strike very much?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, I think two or three times, something like that. I never did like strikes. That threw me out of work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was it worth striking?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I mean, a couple of years ago. Oh, yeah, you had to strike if they, you didn't try to walk through the line or anything, you know. It's the same outfit they got down here. They mean business.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would the company try to make as much off the men as they could?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, I don't know how that is. They used to kind of do that. They wouldn't have to try to make it. I think they're just the same as they are down here. It's the same union. Same union. And everybody's out to get all they can. You know, we have, you know, all these big unions, they had some big fat guy taking in lots and lots of money and giving it over to other big concerns, mines or something. I don't know what they do with it. Well, my father had not too much use for mines. He wouldn't work any. Well, it's all right to work, but they, yeah, I joined up in Lethbridge. Hard work. They said you have to belong to their union. I said, that's fine. And so, joined up right away. Went to work.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And I believed in making good coal. They even told me that I was making too big coal, too big a chance to have to break them. Going coming down through the tip of them, through the screens, just break the screens. So I was about to break them up a little more. I said, that's work. I said, I just want to take them out big the way they are and shove them in the car and you can do what you like with them. And I was pretty asky. I could pick up some very big stuff now. You know, some of these low mines, you'd be picking it up, sitting down, just roll over with it and roll it right into the little cart, you know. Only hold 1 ,400 pounds, maybe.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: So you could load them up and everything. Well, it depends on, you had a long wall system, that was a good system. That was quite a thing. That was around Coaldale, Alberta. They had a wall, I think it was 600 feet. They took everything. And the fire boss, he put a shot every 50 feet or so. You wasn't supposed to put anything in. And when you come in there, you find this coal mill laying down, you see. Maybe. You might find some place that works. And I'd, well, there I could, if it's, they have a, what they call a, oh, I forget the name of that machine now. Sullivan machine. I think Sullivan, it's a machine that cuts underneath. Takes about three inches of dirt from underneath the coal.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear of any of them?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, but it makes sense. And then the fire boss shoots it and it comes down. Well, if it comes down nice, you can split it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the most dangerous thing you ever heard of?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: The ceiling. The ceiling, I'll tell you where the danger, most danger was in these deep mines, like the, up there on the mountain park mine. That's a 45 foot scene. That's a big scene. And that's a gas mine. You wear a gas lamp there. And that's dangerous, but otherwise. I guess if you aren't careful, it's dangerous anyway. I've seen fellas, we used to get under a prop and eat our lunch, and another guy sitting there and so on, feels safe. And I've seen where the roof would break off and come right down on me. But it never did all the time I was there. All my place, my rooms and things was all propped up good and I never, I'm puddled straight. No lean. Puddled straight.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You did your own propping?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh yeah, after all your rooms, if it was 30 feet wide, you had to look after that lunch. I'll see you at the proper price.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How about another guest coffee while you wait?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Sure, usually. Here, that's water's ready.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How about you, Diane?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Okay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what's funny?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: We used to get together and we'd say, gee, I wish I could get a word in that other mine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do I get too strong or just?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: That other working, you know, I get to pay it off with this. They made $14, $16 a day shift.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Gosh, wouldn't it be nice to be over there?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I hope so, yeah. That'd sure be nice. The coal was deeper, you know. Higher seams. And, well, so we talked and they was gonna meet somebody over there and I and another guy or two, we were thinking maybe we'd get him. I don't know. I'm gonna read Mr. King. The other guy, this other guy was, he got it instead of us. And, you know, the two, three days, he was killed in there. My golly, I said, this other fellow, you know, maybe it's a good thing we didn't get that. I said, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He didn't get a chance to make much, did he?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: He just got in there and got killed. You know, one thing I was gonna, that I meant to ask you about that I always forgot to ask, I was talking to Helmer about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why, now your father's name was originally Swenson? Now why did he change his name to Insage?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Listen, if you had six brothers or five brothers and they all flocked around here with the same name, or around in the country. Well, there was more than that. There was all the uncles. Well, come back here. And then, besides strangers, Don again with that same name, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And he just got in business. He got to this. Well, wait a minute. You got it back a little bit. The old grandfather came over. Now this has happened to other people. I know Mr. B. Bam, right out there from Moscow, told me the same experience. The grandfather came over. Alright, his name was Steiner.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, it was Twin, wasn't it?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I know, Ivers Swenson. Ringsogger, S -A -G -E -R. Yeah. That's the immigration. Yeah. That's too long. And, too long a name, and Ringsogger, that doesn't make too much sense. I think you better Americanize it, make it shorter. So the poor guy leaves off the last name. Yeah, I didn't bother. This other family did the same thing. His name was Bjorgson or something. And there they were, I'll get some more. I got them out. Then they went by the name of Ivers Swenson, took out his deeds and everything. Well, and his dad, you see, he had about 11 or 12 kids besides all Steiner's brothers. And all these Swensons. And his dad went in business and insurance business. And, you know, farmers mutual. He started that farmers insurance. And the mail was getting all mixed up, so he decided to swing back to the original name. And then they Americanized it a little. I found it on the map of Norway. It's felt, S -A -G -E -R or S -A -K -E -R. I believe it means circle of metals. So that's how they got the name.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Among your, let's see, your father's brothers and your uncles, was one of those uncles a troublemaker in your family?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, he didn't bother us too much. But I know, dad, you know, he'd come around, he'd come around, I guess one of them. He wanted to get out of the country. And they put up a big story and he had a stud horse or something, I don't know how much it was worth. But anyway, he got $600 out of dad because dad's credit was good. He could just go to the bank, you know, and get it from him. And so he got out, he went east and he was smart. He married a woman back there, with a woman. She put him through college. I guess, well, he was young, he was in his 20s. You know, it was a long time ago. And he graduated as a veterinary. And my gosh, he was really, well he was smart and really good. And Ringman brothers, you know, would come around. They wanted them to send their best student to come around and inspect their horses. And they got this uncle of mine out there. And he knew exactly how to handle them. And he charged them when they wanted to charge them. The main thing was to charge the right price, $200. That was the, they knew he knew something then. But I tell you, he did that. He's the most fat woman that got him through school. Oh, well that was bad, yeah. That wasn't very nice. Full of work. Do things right now, and all that. But as far as, I don't know a thing about his marriage business. I know he married her and she furnished the money. She thought I was smart and that he'd get some place, which he did. And I don't know how she turned out, whether he was needing help, whether she should, he should have helped her, or maybe she was ok, I don't know. Maybe she didn't need his help, I don't know. Well he was the one I guess. But I never liked his way of doing things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he do here? Do you know what he'd done here that got him in trouble?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, only just what I heard. I don't think he got in as much trouble as he pretended. You see, my dad thought well, if he was in that bad trouble, he'd better get out. He'd better give him the money and get him out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he think he'd done? Stung horse or the stud horse?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, the stud horse. He put that up to a prize winner or something. And he believed in it, but he was stung. Horses were cheap at that time anyway. Nice horse, no doubt. I don't know what he was now. He was a pack of horses. He sold him as a prize winning horse. I guess so. I don't know what he told him. He could tell me. He was on a lot of the time. A lot of these things were... I was just a kid then. I know Helmer said that your father was more generous to his brothers than he was to his sons. Oh yeah. Well, even with that, sure. Helmer and I tried to buy a, I was going to say it right over there, a ninety acres of farmland and the rest going down the big canyon. And we could pay it. We was running dad's place. He had a son of two, three hundred acres to farm and we farmed that and did all the chores and everything. And we told him we could farm that other just as well, right in with it. And all he had to do, we didn't have the money, and he'd go to the north. No, he took Ed after a year or two turned us down. But Ed comes over, he had this place. Well, dad offered him a good price for this place, see. And they gave him cash for it. So that he could put on that place over there. And then he went to his note for what was balanced over there. So they were sure of their money over there, see. Now he wouldn't do that or that. He says they got to work for their living. Well, I'll tell you, Ed is a very smart father. I mean, he's very diplomatic. Now when he lived in Canada, he was always on the school board. And they just practically wept when he left. He would move things over, you know, little school places. There's always kids in the parking lot. And he was just down to his act. And he was a good talker. Just really good. Very helpful.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what I was wondering is, do you think that was common for the fathers to favor their own brothers over there?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I don't know. I don't think so. I just think it, I don't know. He was the baby brother, you know, and there wasn't too much difference between them. He didn't try to spend it foolishly. There's no doubt about that. And Ed always, he had a very good logical reason for wanting the money and for doing it. And he didn't drink, he didn't smoke, he didn't crowd around, he was always, you know, looked reliable, time in the church. Oh, he was great on church business. Because grandma was. He went because of his mother, not because of any great personal conviction, but he was pleasing. And he didn't swear and cuss around like you were.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Helmer, you were swearing and cussing, were you?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh boy. No, Helmer, it's not your dozen cuss in the house. Never did, but Helmer, he married Richard Donner, one of the world's little mentors.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: But if it's just, I don't know why, maybe you get so sick of trying to raise kids with your, you raise what you want, you just want to rest away from them. Maybe you argued with him, I don't know why he would act so much like that, because he sure got a lot of mileage out of Steiner and Helmer, he really did. He worked for her, you boys did for him. Oh boy, and just as quick as he could get out, he found out that he could get a talent, you know, for healing and chiropractic work. Well, some doctor or something here told him that he should go to that school, he said, boy, you've got a lot of power. He said, you should go there. And so he finally convinced my dad that he could, and he went there and talked to some of those people, and he liked the school and everything, so I don't know whether he liked it or two turns. Oh yes, he went down, several years he went down. What happened, there was a very sick neighbor, and he was so sick that he needed a couple of men to go with him to Spokane, against the medical doctors had given up, and there was sort of a chiropractor, mental healer, whatever you call it, up there. So they did, they took him up there, and they worked this man over, and this man really was very good. And he talked, he looked at Swen and he said, you've got as much magnetism in you as I have, you could do this. They called magnetism in those days. And he said, I guess Swen must have had a feeling for it, I don't know how old he'd be, your mother was dead, he must have been close to getting on towards 40. speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No. Mother was one of the few. One of the few. But still, a barkeeper's wife wasn't too high status. And perhaps there might have been a few things rumored because it happened, oh, maybe 100 miles away. There's always some female that blah, blah, blah. Mother didn't get it from that woman ever. And yet we knew later on in life. Her sister -in -law told us what happened.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you, matters like this, you know, who you associate with, is that something that would be of concern to your father one way or the other? Or is it just really your mother's problems?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: It was more my mother's problems. Who I married, my father laid the lines on the courtesy, he left, he died when I was 11. And I don't know how it had been later on. You made it clear. He made it clear. He was very, he was very fond of us, me and my little sister, particularly of course my little sister, who was a darling little baby. And he wanted to see us cared for and loved. They were a pretty loving couple. You know, it's amazing the love that existed between people in those days, in spite of all these. Your parents really had a close relationship. A very close, a very loving relationship. And I was going to say, there was such a complete change after mother was a widow. She had been such a gentle, dove -like, little, fluffery woman. You know, a real mid -Victorian. And boy, no, she had to make a living and she became smart, poised, well -dressed. Well, she always was decently dressed, well -dressed before, but she made a point. She was in an office and she was administrating things and she just, I was just dumbfounded myself at 11, 12 to see mother change and assert it. Not faulty, but she was administrating, she was doing things. Just such a change. She was in there all the time and just came out like that. She had men that had wanted to marry her a time or two. She said she never encouraged too many, but she was an attractive woman after my dad died. But she...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She didn't argue or anything?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Mother was very practical.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: There were some, well, she says, once for love is fine, but you've got to look after yourself when you get older.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She said, just what would be an advantage to me to marry him?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: She's a nice guy, but no. I live in that dumb place and I love... She just, she thoroughly enjoyed the city, thank you. She was gone every night. She was free from work. Let's use music. She loved archaeology and politics. She was just into everything, not perhaps so active, but glistening. And then she'd come home just thrilled, telling me all about it. You've got to come tomorrow night, there's going to be space and more. I was just going all the time places between my schoolwork when she was around town. But, later on...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was the first time that she had been in a city, a major city?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes, that size. She'd been in small towns and she'd been in the island. Yes, this was the first time that she... And there was libraries and museums and people talking on the artwork of Indians. You know, all the things that goes on with the colleges. And then there was a very interesting place to live and it was an interesting place to be introduced to in my teens. But then Mother... I was going to go back to Mother and her marriages. There was a man that she'd known before she... when she was back in Prince Edward Island. I guess there were maybe sweethearts after her. She thought they were not too serious. And she came left and he married somebody else. Nothing was said. She knew the wife he married. I don't suppose they even exchanged the Christmas cards. And then he was the widower and so was she. She ought to read those love letters. Beautiful Victorian love letters. They're just gems. I think my sister's got them. Mother says, I'll go back there and live off that Prince Edward Island. She already could take care of me. She already was a nice guy. Arms not bright enough. But oh, the place, the situation. I want more life.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And there was another man. A very nice American man. My father was an American, by the way. And he... this man she met while she was managing another hotel. And a real first -class beautiful hotel over in Westminster. And he'd come up there on a holiday or whatever and stayed several months and got acquainted. And she liked him. He was a very fine person. He was in real estate. And he proposed, gave her a beautiful ring. My sister was still a young girl. I was teaching then. My sister was still in high school. And the wedding was set up. She had a beautiful wedding dress bought. Of course it wasn't anything white or anything like that, but a lovely dress. And my sister got on the boat and went down to I think Los Angeles. There the bombshell dropped. He was in partnership with another man in real estate. A very good business. The children had not met Mother. There was a boy and a girl in a 25 -year -old. And they said, if you marry... they hadn't seen Mother or anything when they heard she was coming. If you marry, we are going to demand our mother share the estate and business. Alright. It would ruin this other man's life. The business was meant for two. It had to be two. They'd have to break it up. They'd have to sell it. Neither one would carry on. Mother left the situation over. She says, at our age, she was around 50 when I was born. It would just ruin both his life and the antagonism of his children. He was called to a big mess. Wrote it up. No tears, no screams, no hope. So many people living like that. But he was attractive enough to talk to him. We talk about... to a good, loving marriage. I think it is. She was better educated than him. Let's face it. He was a good businessman. He was bright and he was strong and all that. And charming. Talk about charisma. He had it. But he admired her intellect. And she did all the business work and all the business writing. I've never heard anything unkind said between them. Of course, as I say, they didn't live together all the year. At times they were together. It was very nice. She always told me how completely considerate he was in every area of her life. Just beautiful, lovely, considerate, loved as children when we came. The two of us, there was a little boy died in between. There's a serenity to her life. There's an anchor. We weren't searching for our identity. I knew who I was. I never... I can't... That's something I guess it's lost on me. I see these poor old grandkids of mine. They're trying to find their identity. You're born with it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now how could you be so stupid to lose it?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: It's there. You know where you are, where you're going.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What is it? What do you lose?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Tell me where's your identity.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you got yours or did you lose it?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Or is it some...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How could you not know?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: You might wish for a few other things. You might certainly not be completely satisfied.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But who is? If you found whatever this mysterious, elusive self is, would you be any happier? What is it? Don't you think I've ever found mine?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, I think for a lot of people it's a search for something to do with your life. I don't know. But it seems to me, when you say identity, that you knew it's for a kid.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you mean by your identity?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I knew I was happy to be a girl. I knew I was going to go up, go to school, I knew I was going to get married. That's what I wanted to do. And I'd like to... There's some things I'd like to have done. I'd like to have traveled a great deal. I haven't got that. I've traveled some, but not as much as I'd like. I'd like this. I'd like to have had more money, actually. Jerry always liked to be a better mother, better parent, better daughter, better everything. But then, there's nothing... I can't think of... I don't know. You know, people say, if I had my life to live over again, I couldn't have done any better. I'd have made some stupid mistakes. Sure, I've made stupid mistakes. Perhaps even a few causal ones, but they're part of life. I mean, there's very few people in your true life about making a few. And you don't find the perfect work. I wasn't too happy about teaching. I think I was... Although that work I did in the nursery was grueling work, it was hard and it had a lot of drawbacks, but I was interested in it. Now I found out, you see, years after I'd had my career, that this is better. I think people have to be willing to work at something for a while and be ready to switch. That's what my mother said. I mean, I was started that way. She would look at her, somebody, sensitive, she'd be, my gosh, that woman's taught for 30 years or 20 years. And this whole big wide world, she says, I'd rather wait table. I'd rather clerk in the store. I'd do anything for, to meet a new class of people, get in contact with something new. I don't mind.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that what she would teach you after four years?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes, yes. She said, she says that this is good. I'm going to be here in this island. She was a very successful teacher, very good teacher. Even in those benighted days, they had a very high standard of education. Prince Edward Island is so small that when you raise a child there, if you raise five children, you know four of them is going to have to leave the island and find work elsewhere. Or somewhere else you start and the minute you have that job preparing them for a life, someplace else, or a career. So, education's good and she had a good job and she had friends and she had fun. But, she says, this isn't all there is to life. I'm going to go west and seek my fortune. She happened to get married, but she quit that hotel business and went teaching there for three years and then swung back again after a while to another similar job. She got older and got tired of that. She even tried nursing, well I mean just caring for people and their homes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think about social class in those days?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: It sounds like your mother was very democratic. Very democratic. She didn't bother her at all in the least. She could be happy, she'd be happy talking to a janitor. She said, you can learn from everybody. And she says, the main thing is talk to everybody. You may learn more from the janitor in the hotel than you will from the owner. And she was most democratic and most kind and compassionate. She was born a hundred years ahead of her time. She really was. She was just felt so badly about so many social conditions that are prevalent now. I think why you see so much, there's so much a much larger population. If you're struggling with this huge population, that's why it's bigger and bigger and you can't feed them. There's more to it than food. They're just exploding all over the place and the community has some time to absorb them and the people to absorb them and nurture them and to love them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm wondering, how do you think she would have characterized herself?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Where she fit in the social scheme of things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think she saw herself as coming from aristocracy?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, no, no, no. We're just working people. But there was a dignity to their class. The island is a unique place. There is an aristocracy. There's a dignity to people that lived in a great many small farms. The farms aren't huge. You're within sight of the sea almost any place. I think it's only a few hundred miles or less long. It's a very small place. Not very many rich people there. No lords or ladies to speak of. You're very much on an equality. There's a few French, because of their language barrier and because of their huge families, you don't feel superior to them but you start to pity them and you know they've got a tremendous handicap. They were quite often the hired men and sometimes the hired girls would come from those families. No, they were very close. You weren't the lowest, you weren't the highest. You're coming out of a community, a lifestyle where all people were very much alike. There were a few fisher people, perhaps that depended mostly on the sea that weren't too well off and had a grim time. There was always a few people that had a... Now for instance, this is rather interesting. There was a hired girl. I guess she worked for my great grandmother. She had a child at a wedlock and she kept that child. She went to work for nothing, for her room and board and the keep of this child for my great grandmother. But I mean, there were things like that. They always called her poor Nellie or poor Ellie or something. Mother said in the end, the girl married all better than most girls. In British Columbia, I've had the impression, and it isn't extensive because I've only been up there a few times, but today in British Columbia I sense a real class distinction. Not to say that it doesn't exist down here too, but it's even more, it's even sharper it seems to me there. I think there is now because we, there's the old waft like my mother. Now she never, there was no feeling of that when she lived, but now the few that's left are just appalled. You're just struggling by these refugees from Hungarian, Poland and hordes coming in from Jamaica or one of those places. But the same thing was going on back then. There were all these refugees from Greece and Italy and all those foreigners. There weren't quite so many. No, there were, we were getting, when I was growing up, there was a few, there was people from Poland and Bohemia and the big cities, there was Italians. But the most of our immigrants were Scotch, Irish, Welsh. They were from the British Isles themselves that were coming over. That was our huge, that was our big population. There was Jews coming into Montreal in the Eastern, they'd never hit Prince Edward Island for instance, you'd never see anything like that there. You would get, oh there was Sikhs out in British Columbia, there was a bunch of them, but there was, they were partly allowed to have wives.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The Sikhs?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes, those Hindus, you know. They were our lumbermen, they did the tree cutting and that sort of thing. But now we've got this huge population, there's just so many of them. And I think, in some cases they're justified. The older people have, well actually they just form sort of an aristocracy. They're a little aloof, they're a little, they get a little irk that these, there's always so many. Just, you go to places downtown, they're just speaking foreign languages. It's so different from when I was a girl, of course that's 50 years ago, when I was going to high school. It's just so different, it's just like a new, a different world out there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why don't you think of, how were Americans regarded in those days when you were growing up?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: People that came, you know like Steiner and others, Americans that came up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How were they regarded?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, I'll tell you now, there's, how it's, now, Prince Edward Island is fairly close to the United States in a way. A lot of our things, like our lobsters and oysters and that are shipped to New York and Boston. When mother was sent to Boston when she was a girl to learn to be a seamstress and she was sent back home again, you know, I mean that was the thing. Send your girl, learn some practical talent or develop some talent that she has. Aunt Amy went there and it was all, the quite, and if you didn't go to college, you went to Boston and learned, Aunt Florence went there and learned to be a cook. A very fine cook and she cooked for establishment, not establishments but for very rich people in Vancouver. Well, they learned to like people, they found, they met a lot of the Irish there and they liked them. And there was some others they liked in Boston. They all, they carried a kindness back with them, a friendliness. Mother had it now, her aunts, mothers, my grandmother's sisters were teachers. They left Prince Edward Island with a brother, there's always, I mean it wasn't a big enough family. And they came out to Michigan, they taught there and they married and they had sort of like a little colony there. And so they were always writing back about we Americans, mother'd write, laugh, she says, boy, she says, have they absorbed, have they absorbed, we Americans feel that this was during the war, that you should have done so and so. They're all, they spoke and they spoke their little minds, but what we Americans should do, mother just chuckle and chuckle. But she liked them, she was very kind. So we had that exposure. But then, up in the Kootenai Lakes, those two summers I was there, we would get Americans from, I suppose, Spokane, the Welk there people, the beautiful resort place, there was a hotel and we'd get them. Mother would sort of sigh, look at that child, look at the clothes she's got on. You can always tell an American, overdressed. That kid is dressed like for a party and here it is Tuesday. Look at her shoes, slippered. Trust me, here I was going to clunk along with boots on and maybe a lemon dress, plain lemon dress, you know, sort of a natural colored thing like that. But we considered them overdressed, loud. We respected them thoroughly for their business ability and for their women. We considered good cooks, good managers and we always admired their plumbing, their machines, their ability to run the machines. That's where the United States is top. I never go by roads that I don't admire dirt moving machines if they're in action. Those are the things. It's sad, I wish you had a few ramblings thrown in, but that's the things that America is just wonderful for. It's too bad she's so kind and never gets any money. I was jarred this morning listening about all that we've given Russia. Not got a nickel for. Oh, I just forget the debt there and mentioned they were hoping they could, no wonder Russia wants detente. I want to ask you about learning to read.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mentioned to me when we were here before, but would you tell me about that again, how you learned to read?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, I was read to, of course, and I just loved it. We had so few books, there was no libraries, there was no place to get books. And I, mother read a few and I went to school and I think I picked, I went to school for two months and I had a little permer. Jane has a dog, Dick loves his dog, Pug. I just hated it, it was dull, I wasn't getting anywhere, I just didn't read it. After all, mother had been reading the most exquisite poetry to me. She would read Longfellow, she would read Shelley, she would read anything. I was being fed this beautiful poetry and then these dumb, Dick and Jane things, the equivalent, whatever they had. And there was no excitement, there was nothing. I always remember that story of the ancient mariner. I'd get my little bathtub, I was only about five, and mother would, it was a too hot mother and she'd put a little icicle in. And then we'd say that the ice was here, the ice was there, you know. And then we'd go out and we'd look at the moon and she'd recite, there was beautiful poetry. About the moon coming up, different about Diana, the moon, and told me all the Greek history. And I wanted her to read to me, I was having trouble, I just wasn't getting anywhere. Then mother told me, she used to tell me stories because she didn't have any other way of giving me literature like that. So she told me the story of black beauty. I begged my aunt, I said, auntie send me a black beauty book. I think she missed the first time and something else, then she finally sent me black beauty. Mother had my little sister by that time and she was busy. She read me the first chapter. Oh, the glory of it, what a wonderful story. I knew, I kind of knew the ending, but oh, there never was any other literature but that. So beautiful, so wonderful, so, I loved animals and that was it. Well, I said, mother read me some more.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She said, why don't you learn to read?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I said, I can't, it's too hard, I can't remember the words. But I want to read, I want to, well, she says try. Let's sit down and see if we can go through one chapter a day together. So we sat down, word by word the hard way. Oh, I knew Dee and Ann, I knew two, maybe I got that far along, but that was it. We went word by word. Of course, actually I got a little better all the time. Didn't sound, no bother, no bother with that, just read. And then some days she couldn't get out and I'd cry. But as it unfolded, I tell you, there's never been another passion in my life like that. There's never been another moment. As I got further into that book, and I could remember, I guess I had a pretty good memory, I could remember the words and I didn't try to sound them out. If I found a difficult word,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'd tell her to tell me, what's this word?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And she tried to make me sound and I'd cry. I was really emotional. I still didn't cry, but I was emotional. I said, tell me and I'll remember, don't keep me waiting. Oh, mother, good old mother, she just came right along, alright, it's thorough. Or it's some other long word of about four or five syllables. And you know, by the time I finished that, I had it late. Then I went on to a novel. It wasn't too complicated, it was called The Story Girl, something like Anne of Green Gables by the author of A Young Girl Growing Up in Prince Edward Island. And there was a few difficult words and I figured them out. And then I was also simultaneously, I got into these history books, the Greek and Roman mythology. They were pretty hard. And then I started putting words together. But you know it would have been torture to take a child, you'd have killed me if you'd have made me learn phonics. When I could, I was so willing and I had such a good memory, you only had to tell me once or twice and I had it. And then I started getting together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So before you were through BlackVue, you were reading it yourself?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I was reading it myself. I was reading it myself and then I just went on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And how old were you?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Nine. Nine coming ten. I started in about January and by February and March I was just really deep in anything. Mother was rather, I don't know whether you should read that book, it's pretty grown up. I think it was the Tale of Two Cities, not the Tale of Two Cities, but Under Two Flags or some of those. I was a little advanced by me, but whatever. It was the biggest thrill of my life I can remember just to this day. It's just like some scientist that worked at some kind of an institute taking their kids and it's yours and it's yours for all life and all time. I wish children could experience that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well then you just kept on reading and reading after that? You became an avid reader?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes. See, Mother always was able to borrow books for herself and she did have books of poetry. We'd get in on that. And then I got acquainted with somebody else that had some fairy tales. A little more like that. And then of course that was nine and then through the years, it wasn't too long until I was in the city and had access.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, these first years between when you were of school age at five or six and nine, you weren't going to school?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, there wasn't no school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Because you were out of town?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Out of town, up there in that mountain. And there was no road, there was just a footpath. And most days people didn't care too much, you know, they didn't exert themselves. Mother says, oh, she'll learn when she gets around to it, when she feels good. She'll learn. I'll teach her when she wants to. I did learn to add and divide and multiply.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she teach you that?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: She taught me that and I asked for it, but I just knew the principle, but I wasn't fast. And to this day I have never learned to spell well because I didn't get it when my memory was at its peak. I learned to write quite well. I mean, not so much for penmanship, but to express myself. Mother started in writing to me when I was up there with my Aunt Een. She wrote to me twice a week and I would answer back. My first letters were dismal, but I was lonely for her and I loved her letters. And she was a beautiful penman and she was just exquisite and she just told me things. Her letters were so interesting that I was inspired to write. You know, we wrote twice a week for at least 25. I was married 25 or 30 years. We were still writing twice a week and we capered off to once a week. We always had something to say and we'd write under any circumstance. She'd write on buses on the back of an old envelope or a shopping bag or a piece of paper she found in the waste paper basket at the post office. And they'd just go flowing back and forth. I don't know what I wrote about up there, not Lonely Prairie, but they'd just, it was just letters. Eight, nine, ten pages at a time, writing back and forth. So I could write and I could read. I'd picked up history through reading and it was just a cinch for me. The only time I was weak in mathematics. I was writing with, she had done, she wrote to a lot of people. She wrote all up there in the back. That was a way for her to actually not be lonely. Oh yes, she wrote. She never expressed loneliness. And we just were so interested in the little things of life like, oh a new moon. We'd just go out there and we'd wait for it. We knew the time. It's coming.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who will see it first?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: It was just a, I don't know, you just have that little prickles up and down your spine. It's there, I've seen it. How lovely it is. You took time to appreciate it. And Mother, she had taken up elocution. That was the correct thing to do, you know, years ago in school. And she could recite some beautiful poetry for almost any occasion. Even if it was the same little poem, beautiful little poem. I mentioned Endymium and Diana, the moon coming up. That was one. She'd tell me these different stories from mythology about the moon. And then the full moons. And the sun rises. You know sun rises and sun sets are very spectacular in the Rockies for some reason. And I always remember eating breakfast and watching it. Watching it and thrilling to it, it's just a physical thing, your response to it. And sun sets. Because there was nothing, there was no competition, there was no radios, there was nothing. It was just nature to respond to. Daddy loved nature. He always picked beauty. The place, the house wasn't much, but you were surrounded by beauty. You just absorbed it, you expected it. And there was flowers, they're very very beautiful there and there's lots of them. So your mother really accepted his inclination to be poor but in a beautiful place. Well, he wasn't actually poor when they married, but that was his lifestyle. He would not put money in a house. The house was just a warm shelter with plenty of food. He house -proud people and people that had elegant houses. Now look, he had two houses in town. Just one town and he owned a delivery staple and he had an income from that. But he wouldn't live in those houses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: See?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: He liked it up there amongst beauty. By himself. He liked it up there. And the isolation possibly and if people wanted him bad enough he'd come on and talk. I wonder why it would have made him that way. When most men were interested in living in town they weren't part of it. Well, he was kind of hobos I guess. Well, he wasn't a hobo, but I guess there's always been free souls like that. And you know half of it. I don't know whether I told you, but he really in a sweet way, in a nice way, I never felt the harshness of it. Oh, there was a little ruffle or two, but I guess the girls and the women wore high heels, you know, and these button shoes and they were kind of tight. And he was determined his little daughter would have beautiful feet. And I wore boys boots, I'll have you know, till I was, till he died. Accepting on Sunday. Boys boots, the kind that you have, you take out the laces and get some good old moose hide or some kind of elk hide. You trim them down for me, put them in, they won't be breaking all the time. They're always about two sizes too big. And here I go plunking around happily and then barefooted in them too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So your feet didn't get bound up?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: They never grew too big and they have, even now, I've got beautiful straight toes that I thank him for. The associates of my mother's, they had bunions and corn and here I had lovely straight feet. But I mean not every kid could take that, but I was up there and it didn't matter up there in the mountains. And he rather liked, he couldn't stand Indian women because they were so dirty. He was, being on the island, you couldn't help but brush with these Indians and Indian tribes. But he thoroughly approved of their foot gear. And he thought they were dressed nice and he liked their modest way, two braids of hair. He said that's the way every woman should be. That was the days of pompadours, rats. All those pompadours just didn't get that way. You had batcombs and you put a rat, what they called a rat, inside to hold it up. The fury of them. That's no wonder he didn't think women should have a vote, any woman that could do that. But a nice modest woman would have, she'd wear the gear, foot gear of an Indian woman and have some of the makers and modussons wear her hair in braids. And of course that was another thing. Because women really cinched themselves in. These laces, I remember lacing his sisters up, you know, getting them in tight. Oh, he wouldn't be there looking but he'd know what was going on. Tighter, tighter, I can't get that dress fastened, Vina. Tighten it up a little bit more. You women better cut that out, you're going to ruin your health. You'll be having problems.
