words: Well, three squares now. You start like this, you know. You get the distance here. And then you get the distance this other way. And you add them together. And they give you this distance. So that you can work from this over to there.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Anyway, well... I just wouldn't figure it out. I had it in my job, but I never had it. I just explained it to the guys. And I was always right that way, so it worked out alright. But I think it would have been a good idea for me to hand in my work. But I was too busy retaking tract and all that stuff. I made the team, the five mile tract team, and spoke in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you tell me a little bit about this place itself, this house, when it was first built?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, well, gee, I wouldn't know much about it when it was first built. But Finch did a 19 -1. Well, yeah, and I was 17 -18.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was kind of an old house then, wasn't it?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: When I'd be 17 -18, it would be kind of an old house then. Oh, it wasn't so old. Well, we've got a sign out here that says 1900. That's when they moved in. Yeah, in the fall. But they said they had the old homestead cabin before and after. Yes, down there between. It was between that shed, that barn -like thing we have, and where the old cobwein was. This was the second year through that. And this was your grandfather built. And you saw, I don't know if we ever showed you the picture or not, but this was taken, you know, even before they had the house built. One of the first things, there was a man came through here selling fruit trees. And they had, I guess, the orchard even set up before they had the house built. Now this is the house about, I thought this would be, what would this be, 1910 or something, or even before then. See, we don't have the, this was a fruit cellar. Look at the good orchard they had. So you see they have. You know, they built the orchard before they built the house. That's why they got one tree there so close to the house, it was only about two, three, four feet. And you know, when that tree got this big around, it hit the apple tree. It broke down on the house, well it couldn't hurt the house. But then I had to take it away. But I wouldn't until it just broke and leaned right on that. Now these are logs, chinked, and that was one long one like when we came here. There was just the one window here and we tore it out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you see, you see how many windows I got out of it?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Now see that one there, it was up and down, there was two windows. Well I took and cut out a bunch of logs here and put four in the breast. And then you can see all around. I know my uncle looked and he was, gosh, he was making a lot of difference. And then when you looked around like this you saw that chicken house that's on there. Yeah, this was the new chicken, this was the chicken house right out there, the plum tree. Yeah, not the chicken window.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was the same wood shed, you see out there?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And that was the pool barn then, now this part here is blowing down. So I took that away, now that was a good chicken house. This was a chicken run here, this was buried, it was quite very discreetly put there. You grew up, you went over to Central Ridge when you were just a little pop. Oh yeah, I was coming sixth in the spring.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you moved over?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I moved over there I think in March, and I was five, coming sixth. Sixth and last of the bay, 31st Bay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was your father's place right near here? Well, what we lived here, you mean?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well we lived a half mile up in the mountain there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know where you turn in there?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: You go up half a mile to the right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't that the other way?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah, Catherine was built before we came here from the east. And the guy that built it and lived in it, he first thought he'd squat on some land here, see. Then he got another idea, that he'd get out, somebody's talking, getting out and getting someplace else. So he just walked off, left. So then the neighbor said, well, you know I had an uncle there, there was quite a few relatives. Some around here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And this is, well, what's the name of the house? The house is empty, why don't you just move in there, see?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: So alright, we moved in there. And was in there for four years, I think it was. Five, let's see, something like that, four or five years. Well, it'd be five coming sixth as I was in there, because... But anyway, I was about six months old I figured. I was born 31st of May and we moved up there. And as near as I can figure out, it was in the fall.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And it was that fall that I was born. No, no, you were born in the spring, dear. Oh, the fall of the year you were born. Yes, I was born in the summer, you know, May. I'd say that's summer, or spring. And then I was born in the fall, so I says, it must have been November. One time. Well, I says, I must have been about six months old then. So we stayed here a year or so, and then mother wanted to go back east. She got homesick or something. And she went back east and stayed a while. Had twins! Let's bring out the truth, she had a twin back there. She had to go back and have them, so they were born in Minnesota. So Minnesota must be a wonderful place. You had to even go back there. You know, I talked to her old aunt, his old aunt's mom back there, and she remembered the dear little twins being born. And you know, I thought it's nice, people did such nice things. Here she was, Helmer and Steiner, you can just imagine, they wasn't any better than they are now. And then these little twins, and they sent her brother, one of her younger brothers out here, to help her out. She sure needed it, you and Helmer and the twins, baby boy. Oh yes, well, she had to, that was one of the right things, too. They had to put out the money, too, you see. Mother didn't have the money, dad didn't have any money. But I thought that was very nice of them, to give her a little help and hands. Oh yeah. Well I think my mother's people were very nice, that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What, are you going to speak?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I was going to ask how it was that you became a teacher. Well you know, there was only two things to do when I grew up. Be a nurse, or be a teacher. Well, mother's a teacher, and I thought it was the easiest of the two. Actually, I think I'd have made a good nurse, oh, nursing was hard to learn. Nursing was so rugged in those days, those twelve hours the nurses would put in the scrubbing. You did all the work that the aides and the training women do now. You scrubbed the beds after a patient left. Oh, they filed the work off. And it was pretty hard, so I thought, oh, I'll teach. So nursing was tougher. Oh, it was tougher, just to learn. Well, it's not as bad as it used to be. Teaching, I think, is tougher now because discipline is... Teaching is nothing. It's discipline and little vandals and upset parents, neurotic kids. I didn't go to talk to the little vandals because they're mine. Just let me tell them a minute about teaching. In those times there was quite a demand for teachers, too. There was the prairies and all these one room schools. That was, I mean, you were more sure of a job. That was another factor. And besides, think about, you get a better choice. You get a swing at the boyfriends. See, I lived at the end of a golden era. Just at the end. The beautiful golden age where women were prized. They were scarce. There was scarce commodity. This was just after World War I. And there was about four or five bachelors to every girl. You'd go out there to those little country schools. Boy, there was never a debitage at any better time. All these men competing for you, you just had to be a female. You didn't have to dispense with your favors. You could be prim, you could be modest. You were alone. That's all that was necessary. And you got all this attention.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It's pretty nice, wasn't it?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, it sounds great.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this just, do you think this was just true in the backwoods areas like that?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes. No, it wasn't the same in the cities, but out in the prairies. Out in the prairies in the backwood areas. Well, nobody wanted to live there, you see. Well, there were all these bachelors just starting up. And all these men, there were Scotchmen, Englishmen, and whatever coming out from the other countries.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have a lot of men pressing their attention on you?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, definitely. You go there to a... The center of everything was the little country schoolhouse. And there'd be dances in some schoolhouse somewhere. Well, you'd have a... there's usually... there wasn't too many cars, but you'd usually latch on to somebody. He might not be the one you wanted, but he'd have a car. And he was wise enough and lucky enough to not press his luck. He'd take you there and he'd get the first and the last dance and maybe the dinner dance. And he had to be satisfied. Then you'd have dances. You'd maybe be booked up for four or five dances a head. And people wanting dates. And he was just, at the end of the time, sorting them out. Times were so... I don't know, the girls have to do so much now. And to interest people. It seemed to me that unless she's an awful good brain, or awfully wise or awfully pretty or very shrewd, but there you just had to do so little. Just be natural and talk and just give a little companionship and listening and you had it made. I don't know, the girls now, they seem to me they have to go more than halfway. Not every time, but so many times.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were there and single like that, were you seriously hunting for a husband then?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: You were looking, definitely. You weren't hunting. You were sorting, shall we say. Keeping an eye. You were keeping an eye open. The only thing that kept me going... And then another thing, it was interesting. Different backgrounds, different men, different backgrounds, young. And past the time, too. I met him, he had a house, and he had a good line, an excellent line.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Excellent line?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, you bet. One of those slick Americans, you know. Yes, you mean. I guess that's what was put into the line. I had a two bedroom house. Well -built. Warmest house in the country. My uncle built it. And the floor, my old George and my uncle's father -in -law, he wanted to stay with me here. And my gosh, he was already talking about getting too old, you know, to cook and keep up. Well, I thought to myself, gee whiz, I better get married. I can't batch. So he had, I think he did it first. But he could cook the nicest pancakes you ever saw. He was a German cook, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Gosh, he says, you know how many pancakes you ate this morning?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, five or six. Yeah, he said thirteen, and they were big.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you going to say?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I was going to ask how. I asked him, tell me a little bit before, but I'm real curious to know how you did it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you get married?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, that was simple. I'd worked at a summer school. You know, some schools went there from March till January, all through the summer, to avoid the bitter cold weather so the students wouldn't have to go out. Well, that's the first kind of school I had. Well, I got out in January and I could have gone back, but it wouldn't have been till March. Or maybe part way into March. So, dear, I hate to lay around, I had an aunt there, but I saw this ad in the paper and I broke in and I got it. And so I came and, well, let me see. The first week I was invited out to a dance with a, that was that Catholic boy that I felt guilty about. Nice boy, and he went down to one of the, not Coron, but somebody else down at Gilt Edge. Anyhow, I went down there and Sunday we went, the next Sunday, that was Friday, Sunday I went to those, the horse traders place. My landlady drove me there and on the way back she says, it's kind of cold, it's January, let's drop by Steiner and warm up. Yeah, she was my guardian. Yeah, she was trying to get him married off. She was an English woman. Every time she got a new teacher, she'd invite me over for a walk. Actually, she was an interesting person, she was a boor from Africa. And so we dropped by there. Beautiful clean place and coastal school and so on and so on. And, all right, very smiley and very nice looking and lovely blonde hair and, you know, the healthy outdoor muscle guy. So, all right, time we didn't stay long. Time to put on my galoshes. It's right there to put them on, and he's watching. A little trouble getting my glove done. He was there to snap it for me. All right, nothing sad, you know, but you were watching all the time. So then he called me up and asked me if he could take me to a dance the next Saturday and I said no, somebody else had asked me. And, well, he was there. And he, then I guess she asked for the next Sunday's dance.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: But, he took me out once then. Took me out on the, I guess she took me out on the Sunday afternoon, or the next Sunday afternoon. Well, after you came down, Goldin brought you down. Well, the next Sunday. I made the date that. For the next Sunday afternoon to take me for a drive. Well, he took me for a drive. I went to, Friday I went to a dance with somebody else, so I saw him there. Sunday he took me for a drive, the next Friday he took me for a dance and he proposed to me. That's what I liked about him. A laggard's in love, you know. A laggard's in love in a dastard and war. Well, near oneness with a fair element, young, locking, baw.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know that story?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Where you all learn. These laggards in love that never know what they want. I want someone that knows what they want from me, not right now. Not saying I'm going to marry them, but I want them to be attracted enough to be willing to sign on the daughter's line. He was, right then and there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you accept him right there?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, of course not. Good grief, no. But then you don't even intend to. Well, you got the upper hand right away. Oh, sure, well that's the thing. You know, people around there say, hey, why don't you come out to the dance, you know. That was the first dance they had after she came out. Well, I said, heck, I don't know. I said, they don't interest me, those screw moms. The more they come, the worse they get. Yeah, I said, the worse they look, and I just don't give a darn about them. I thought sometime I'd come down to the States. It wouldn't take me long to pick out some of the girls. I knew pretty well down there. And so, oh, they thought that was fun. Mrs. Golding brought her around to the house, and you know George kept the house good. We were there several times. In that front room, you know, shiny. He used some of this oil of cedar to put on that nice chlorine. Oh, he brought the grain of the wood out nice and it looked good. And warm. Heck, we had a pretty alien there, I guess. And that'll attract anybody. In Canada. Well, you know, I was very...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You guys discovered them, did we?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, you didn't know. But you know, I was very practical. I realized then I could have ten men. I'd seen enough of them, and know I could make it go as married as anyone. Providing they had a home and a few, just a few of the essentials of life.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, so what?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: There was no young fella around that had anything to say. Well, there was, no there wasn't, I mean, they were all struggling. No matter where I went, they were all struggling young men, but they were good men. I worked hard for what I had, and neighbors had always watched what I got. And I know when I left up there, our biggest farmer up there, he come over and asked me, he says, I got a lot of land to break up. He knew I was breaking ground every year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he said, what kind of plow do you use?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And I told him, I said, I've been using, I've had cock shut, John Deere, and different kinds. And I says, I've got another notion, I want the International. And I told him the reason, and I said, you get a, I think they're only 20 inch. But I says, and I only use ten horses, you put on twelve, you've got lots of horses. And I said, you can walk right along all day through anything you've got over there. And you know, he did that, and he plowed 80 acres of new ground. Well, of course, he had plenty of help, they could have it brushed, brushed as fast as they could plow. He gave you advice. Oh yeah. So, we, Raffle was his name. I got my, we got our name in that other book, you haven't got it here. It's in the front room somewhere.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You, what?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, I was going to say, those golden days are over, the schools are all consolidated, they have the same hassle, the same problem, a lot of that fun. But I was just being, you know, the fringe, it was fun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Steiner, did you know that you were interested in her right away, as soon as you met her?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Just about, right away, yeah. We agreed on everything, pretty much. Well, but I mean, before you knew that, you already put her galoshes on and buttoned her gloves for her. Oh, and I'd do that with anybody. He's more daring than Helmer. That Helmer, he'd go here at his age, he went to a few of these senior citizens, and we asked him over, did he have a pretty nice meal there, and we went, oh, I can't stand it. Those women are always asking me over for dinner, and I wouldn't think of going there. He is just as vulnerable, and he's just as frightened, he's just as skittish, as if he was some young teenager just about to be trapped. He is cute. No Steiner is as polite, but I think he was attracted. Well, I'd had girls, I'd be dancing with girls, and they thought they were so much better and all that, boy, heck, I could just let them go just like that, and I wouldn't ever ask them for another dance.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you know if they thought they were better than you?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, that's easy. That's easy. You know, I think contact dancing teaches you something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think? Have you never had much of all that?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: They were too good. Not as much anymore, but... I really think that some of the romance in life, I think they're getting back into it. He's an excellent dancer, and I was pretty good too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who's that?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: That's a good dancer, dear. I used to be. Pretty good. Oh, you're a real dancer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what's good about contact dancing?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I don't know what there is about it. It isn't an intimacy, but there's something about it. Well, I just think it's your awareness. I think just little imperceptible things about a person when you're that close, that you can pick up and learn and feel and sense and what you say. These faraway dances where you're just... No wonder people feel so solitary, so lost. I think there's something very romantic about those old -fashioned dances. Waltz and Shaw, and well, Shawditch is more action things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I still want to know how you know that if a girl thinks she's superior to you, how can you tell that from dancing?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, what she says while she's dancing is the way she talks. It's what you sing, it isn't what you dance. It isn't the dance itself. It's the conversation. It sets up an eye to eye very close and it is. There's one girl up there, a school teacher. My God, Mrs. Golding, she expected me to do the right thing. I went out to the barn and got a couple of saddle horses, saddles, and I brought them up to the house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which one do you want to ride? Which horse do you want?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, I'll take this one. Okay, fine. She took that one. So I was supposed to go with her. We're going for a ride. I said I'll take this one. So we go on, or no. So I thought I'd help her out. I helped the horse, I was holding the horse, you know. And she grabbed the horse by the riser stall or something, I guess she was used to riding. And I wanted to take her foot, you know. Give her a boost. Whether you need it or not is the correct thing. And there she says, I can get on my stuff, I don't need you around here. I just smile, you know. I said, all right, so get on. So we got on, we rode on five, six miles. Not a darn word said. So I said, let's run a race.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you say?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Okay. She agreed to it. I ran off and left her.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I stopped, waited for her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What are you doing back there all the time?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: You're not kidding. And she got to talking, but she wouldn't talk before that. But she kind of woke up then, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But now, girls like that, all the different ones, isn't it?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: That was Blair's wife. She's the first one to come in. I know there's some that try to throw their weight around when they're at dance or something. When she come in, you know, she thought, oh, everybody's just falling for her. Gee whiz, yeah, but they, she had to look around. She had to be careful she wouldn't get a dance. The only thing, the reason why I danced with her, I'd like to dance one dance with her, because she was from our district. And anybody from some other district, they wouldn't have danced with her, see. They didn't want her. It was a little adequate. You see, I had to show her that I didn't turn it down altogether. I was from the same district she was from.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which district is that?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: School district.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was his dance at? Wainwright?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, no, in the schoolhouse. It was right in the 14th and Wainwright, in our own little school. And then Plaxstone. Plaxstone, every little school has their dancing, their tours with fiddlers, some do this.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would people come from very far around or just, or just right?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, every school, every five miles I think it is. Maybe some of them, if it was a particular attractive girl or somebody they wanted to see, they might come as far as 10, 12 miles. Depends on the girls. Oh, yeah, you can go further than that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there a lot more men at the dances than girls?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, that's the joy, that's the fun part. Yes, there'd be maybe three to four to five girls, depends on your district. But that's all gone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you, so when you turned him down at first, what did you say? I mean, did you?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, I just, well I just told him down, I just said I'm sorry, I have an engagement for you next Friday, I'm going to such and such a schoolhouse. Well, you said he, but he offered to marry, he asked you to marry him. Well, that didn't make any difference, I just, if you had an appointment to go with someone else, you wouldn't break it, just because a guy asked you to marry him.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I mean, you just don't break anything. He wouldn't expect me to, even if he didn't. Well, you see, I was going...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wasn't going with him or anything, he hadn't, you know what I mean?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I was getting... No, there'd be nothing established between... I was getting desperate because old Georgia began to fail.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had you asked a lot of girls that summer, or?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, no, he wasn't, he wasn't. No, I never asked once. I wasn't in any hurry, I just played the field for a little longer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So did you?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, yes, we didn't get married the last spring, later on, I got sick of my boarding house and the kids, all the kids were hard to go with. Well, I had a girl school. So there was this big boys, you know, and there was about five great big boys. I had a girl... I was planning on getting married, and I thought, oh, shoot, I might as well get married. I had a girl ever since I was a little old fella. We was... Oh, and we was going to the public school, you know. I said, I don't know how old I was, but anyway, she was six years old. And her brother, you know, he just didn't like to have, to look after his little sister. He had another sister older than him, you see. So he come over, he says, Donner, here's a girl for you. And he gave her to me, he says, that's your girl. I says, okay, fine, Carl. So I took her. And she was so glad she got all the love I had, you know. And when we grew up, we wrote letters for, oh, I don't know, two, twenty -five, thirty years. I had it on the mind all the time. I didn't give a darn whether I ever got anybody else. And she never got married. And she always said, I told her, I said, well, you get somebody that's got money and got a good place, get married. You didn't know, she's just beautiful. I don't know why you didn't marry. You didn't have an upside down. I told her, I told her, I told her finally, I says, well, don't look like you're going to get married. I says, I know I can't. I haven't got even the money, I said, buy the license plate. And I said, you know what times are now, you better go ahead and get married. She did. She did. Well, I think you'd be a pretty darn well -written seller. Yeah, I think you did too. I'm satisfied. He's more awful good, you'll have to admit. You know, durability. But she figured that something else happened, I just didn't want her or something. That's that Ethel that you went with. She was pretty. She was pretty not only that. You answered, we were talking about. She did me a lot of good turns too, you know. If I went with a girl around that district and she had said something, you know, about me or so, and it'd come to her, she'd tell me. She wasn't allowed to go with anybody outside of the, away from her folks, you know. She was 16. And when we met in any school or any place, and she'd say, you're going to such and such a place. Of course, she knew every girl I was going with. She said so and so. Tell me all the news. And I never told it back to anybody, so we had that between us all the time. It sounds like her parents were awful strict and they wouldn't let go of anybody. No, not strict. That was unusual. I never went out until I was 16. 16 was the age. That was the accepted thing. And you know, she left and went to California when she was 15. Coming 16. So that was it. You know, you're.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were you starting to say?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, I'm surprised we were talking a while about divorce.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, one of the things, now we're speaking of dances. You went with your husband. You were married. Fine. That was an achievement. You had all these young herd of stampede and hopeful around you. All right, they retired. They knew their place. They retired quietly to the background, but still they enjoyed you. They liked to dance with you and talk with you. It was something. Your husband, like he was a very nice husband, he dutifully danced the first dance, the separate dance, and the last dance with me. In the meantime, you had this ball scampering around. If he wanted to dance, fine, but he would usually find somebody to talk the plows with. Of course, he danced a lot too. But here you go. The dumb enough kid talks and they stand up. They go to a dance and dance all night with one guy. You got them when you're going home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the next? You want to dance some all night?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I don't understand it. But you have that freedom. And you meet this man they're talking. And I remember those garno men they were talking about. Politics. Good old George. We used to dance to shotty sets, but old George was good for it. And he'd be talking about some interesting Ukrainian thing. You had all these contacts. And yet they were innocent. There was nothing out of the way. And you were above reproach and it was fun. You weren't worrying about cousins or divorces or entanglements. You knew that married guy you were dancing with was tied up tighter than a drum and you're not about to take him or anything's going to happen. I think you feel more secure. It may not be as exciting, but sometimes you like a little security. Another interesting thing I was thinking about the other day is just a matter of names. Not that it matters greatly. You were asking me the differences between here and in Canada. It's the matter of your first name. Now, I went to public school. Up until the time I hit grade 8, I was dean. By the time I got into grade 8, I was 13, 14, something like that. I must have been 14. Suddenly all the men and teachers, there was two or three that came in for different things. You became Miss Wilson. The boys, in a way, they were called by their last name. Jones, Balinski or whatever. No more Bobby or Jimmy. Something dignified. The woman teacher, she would continue to call you by her first name. When you got to high school, unless she was a very old woman, an older teacher, she would call you Miss. Even from someone that was old enough to be your mother. It was just a little sense of identity. You were a woman and you began to feel it. You began to feel that respect from your men teachers. Boys, all right. Even amongst the girls, there would be a few girls that would call you by your first name. Usually they would call me Wilson. Hey Wilson, come here. Just like you would in a barracks or someplace else. More so than my first name, unless it was some real closest friend. Then when you became married or when you went on, my landlady would never call me anything else but Miss. Pupils, Miss. The men, now, there was just a little variation. I would call George, this famous George, George. He would never call me anything but Miss. Ringsage to this day. Yeah, always. And my women friends, I don't think there was hardly any woman out there ever called me by my first name. No. It was something separate.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I don't resent it. It doesn't bother me, especially all those years down. I don't know about just Peggy. Peggy might have called me Jean in due time and her husband, but we never, we taught the most intimate things with one another. A man would kiss me, a young man would kiss me before he married me, but he'd still call me Miss Wilson.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: That would never be after marriage. Even down here, it's more familiar. Well, of course, I came into a district here where we were related to every last person here. Just practically solid intermarriage. And you, well, relations, you just couldn't expect anything else but Jean, and that was it from here on at the end. And it's true, excepting when I was working at the nursery. There you became Misses. And amongst the other married women.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did Laura call you?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: She called me Mrs. Ringsage. Always, never. Let me see. She was the supervisor of the nursery. Always, and none of the nuns called me anything else but Mr. Ringsage. And the girls were West, using, Turner, Peterson.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where's Peterson?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: You know, we called, but they still would never intrude. In other words, it was the same there in the formulas. Because they were very fine, as I say, they ran over with a fine tooth comb. You couldn't smoke or drink. I swear, I mean, they really were fussy about picking you out. But she called you Mrs. not, she didn't call you Ringsage, she called you Misses. But other girls were called by their last name. No, other girls, we called each other. No, no, the other girls were called Misses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think the significance of that is?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I don't know. I just wonder. I often wonder about girls in particular.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Does it give them a little more sense of their worth as a female?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: A little dignity, a little, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What does it signify to you?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: It gave me a little feeling, a little jolt. Boy, I'm nervous now. Yes, I mean, I'm nervous. It's just a little shade of honor, respect.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many people pay you respect now? How many teachers pay respect?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And it was a nice way they would say it. It just automatically was persuaded.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were just little wobbly kids yet?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: You did little things. I was, I was far out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I was the first girl in my grade in the whole school that bobbed her hair. Now there you can see that I really turned the world over. But you see you had to do so little in those days to really...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was a little radical of a time to do that? To bob your hair?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: There wasn't anybody else. There was hardly any women with bobbed hair. I remember there was Irene Castle. She was the one. She was that famous dancer with her husband, Irene, and whatever his name was, Castle. And she had bobbed hair and I thought it was very interesting and beautiful. My hair was a mess anyhow. It was scraggly and short and thin and nice. So I cut it. And then there was another girl in the class. And poor thing, now I realize what she went through. I don't know whether... It must have been... I wouldn't say it was... She didn't seem to have Negro features and she was fair, but there are other races that have that problem, but her hair was very fuzzy and short and she always had a broad band of ripping around and tied in front. Then when I liberated myself, she did. And then we did naughty things too like...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did she do? She took the ribbon off?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: She took the ribbon off and let her short hair be thin because her hair just naturally didn't grow. So you see, we had ways of being defiant, exciting. And a lot of us, we rolled our stockings below our knees. Of course, our dresses were long. Now stop looking so shocked.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Rolling the stockings below the knees was shocking?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, definitely. That was really, really... Of course, your dress, for goodness sake, your dress came halfway down your side, but then there was always a hope there'd be a breeze and going up a step or across your knees. Some you might see in a little dimple of you, so there. And that was something that was rather frowned on, but not quite for a didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You started to say something else, didn't you?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, I don't know just what, but there were some little things like that. And that was rebellion. That was rebellion and defiance. You have to do so much now with pitiful to be rebellious and to be defiant.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: They didn't try to stop you or anything, but they raised your eyebrows. Of course, that's one little thing. You couldn't see it half the time. I didn't do it after the first initial shock of about six months. It was comfortable having your softens up again. Well, when you went out as a teacher, were the rules very strict that governing what a teacher... Oh, yeah. You knew it was expected of you, and you complied easily and naturally. You wouldn't be any role -soulful in those times. Now, for instance, my first dance I went to. Now, I'd taken my dad's advice, and there were no girdles confined in my natural waist. Of course, I was slim and quite a nice little figure. But I knew there was sort of a thing. Women particularly well brought up nice women always wore some kind of a corset with definite stays in it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I asked my landlady, I said, what will I do?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I'm going to a dance. I said, I haven't got a girdle with stays in it. I don't know. I had some kind of a little deal of Hellmestock and stuff. Well, she says, gosh, I don't know. I wouldn't want anybody to talk about you. They might think things, you know. We talked it over, and finally she loaned me a pair of hers. Gotta be careful. So, I guess maybe I only did a few times, and I found most of the girls in the district weren't wearing girdles with stays in them. Forget them.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: But I'd come a long way from my mother. My mother was telling me of her trials with the teacher. Oh, she's the one that had misery. But she was right, vivacious and full of fun. And, I mean, she was innocent too. I mean, she was nothing,

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: nothing, she should, nothing but, nothing in her life that you wouldn't expect of a woman that was beyond reproach. But she said she had a walk to a field back and forth to school. And of course the long dress was high top shoes. They used to just get soaking wet in one particular miserable November. She came home, feet cold. They didn't have galoshes and things in those days. And her dress was wet. So she, and there was no heat upstairs in her bedroom where she boarded. So she took those wet shoes off and of course she didn't have them. I don't think she had another pair of shoes. If they were, they weren't too suitable. She didn't want to put her wet shoes on. And she sat down in front of the fire and she just brought her dress up so that her feet could dry out. Her landlady came in and she says, Or, that would not be proper to be without shoes and a skirt above your ankles. My husband is in the house. So I thought I got off light. Mother said she could never forget that. The last of me to be murdered was much more liberal. Much more willing to fight for women's rights and family planning and all these things that's coming. She ate, she must have been an animal. Oh she wasn't. She was a poor little hen -pecked man and she just, Mother says, The poor guy didn't hardly dare lift his eyes above his plate when she ate her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, did your mother actually become active in those sort of times?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, it wasn't, it hadn't progressed. It was just barely starting up when I was a girl. But I mean all our sympathies were with, not only women, but men. Men we needed liberating too. You know we've got so much going for us. We've got this nice little extra layer of fat, live longer, and just think of all we've got going for us now. So that's why I think you need liberating. You know, you were talking about, you were talking about women as actually having a better position in a way because they didn't have to work. And when you said that, I was thinking about that, it seemed to me that women had to work pretty hard in the home. They had to work tremendously hard in the home, but all working in a store. So that 12 hour day, that 12 hours and miserable bosses, and poor pay, and as I say, even the hospital worked so hard, even the respectable jobs. No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But compared to the work that the husband was doing, didn't women still have to work pretty hard at home?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: She did, but you see I have a picture of my dad being gone most of the time, and my mother having it very easy at home. I never saw a woman working too hard. Grandmother managed to have a hired girl, she had eight kids, but still she had a hired girl. See, mother didn't have to work much at home when he was gone to keep the place up. That's right. And when he was home he was very helpful. I remember he did a lot of the working because it was done on the board. He didn't mind helping, and he'd hang clothes out in those cold Canadian winters, he always hung the clothes out. And he'd pack the water, and he'd always kept the fire going. She always said she had it pretty easy. And that's all I ever, that's really, and my aunt, Aunt Amy, she had it the same way. My other, she was another adored wife, and my aunt Frances, boy she was just spoiled, well I shouldn't say she was spoiled, but she was another one. She was the kind of woman that got her a $900 coat, for a coat during the Depression, so she wasn't doing so bad. Her husband had loved her, and she didn't have too much work. So I never saw the real hard slaving women. I knew they were around, but if you didn't have too much of a family you got by. Another thing, there was a lot of things. Your washing wasn't so much. You had one outfit of underwear all week, none of this, twice, once or twice a day. You used a towel for a week, and you could use a bath towel cheerfully for a week. Everything was on a weekly basis, unless you fell in a pond or something. It eliminated a lot of things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: See, like when you were first married, in those early struggling years, did you feel that you had to work a great deal, very hard?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I know because I never did any outside work. Loneliness was my big problem. Loneliness and lack of contact, as I say, I yearned for the city, not for a lot of things, not for social so much as the intellectual. Mother kept my mind alive, rather died. She sent me books, she sent me magazines, she sent me word by word descriptions of what was going on, and she had a certain line of Bible study she was interested in, something you've never heard of. The Re -Shizreh Lites, there was a real wing -dinger up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What is it called?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: The Re -Shizreh Lites. Mother was deep in that, it kind of fell by the wayside, but they were always digging out things. There was a lot of archaeology, and Mother was sending the most fascinating things, but they dug up this and that, and stone hinge and all the whole thing was put together. So what you felt was cultural isolation. That was the whole thing. It wasn't hard work. I mean, I was young and I didn't have my children, although the first two came close, the next had space. It wasn't hard work as a staff. That was the prime thing. Joseph. There wasn't really anyone that you could talk to that gave you the mental stimulation. There was a few teachers, yes, there was quite a few married teachers, but not too many. Politically there was a few. There was some excellent people I could disagree with politically. That was a help. That I could disagree with. They were much in earnest and they were quite smart and quite bright, and that was a help. But the women, I had some good women friends, some, well, fairly well educated, pretty good, but they were isolated like me. They were isolated from the stimulation that they needed. They weren't too happy either. That was the whole thing. There was a whole area that was just sort of blotted out. Although there was lots of other things. We had things that you didn't have down here to at least keep your mind, the wheels turning over. There was much more involved in politics. Just ever so much more.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And social issues. You were always just very involved. For instance, the government came out with a rule that a bill went through that in order to save money, we advised that women have their first child at home to save on money. And you know, things like that. And then you tear into them. Something like that. You could really get your teeth into that and fight back. But they did. They actually did. And I came out with some of the papers. Some of these districts, I mean the municipal districts. This was during the Depression. Anything to cut down on bills and save a dollar. Why women have the first child at home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the sorts of, the basis that you would disagree with some of these people on politics?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: They were too far to the, no they leaned towards communism. That's what it was going, they didn't say it was communism, it was going to lead into that. There was too much socialism attached to it. I didn't like it. It wasn't a matter of liking or disliking it, I could see the flaws in it. I got pointed out, at times, good God is right. So these were socialists and not conservatives that you were arguing with. Yeah, and I wasn't conservative either. Alberta is really the most progressive and they're not, they're conservative in some ways but their politics are much more liberal and much more progressive than the other provinces around there. They, oh for instance, they have a, they've got a good firm grip on their oil. The province has it, it isn't all in the hands of Texaco. They may have a little, they dabble in it alright and people do make a little money out of it but the province has a lot of the revenue from it. And they're putting it into a heritage tax, a heritage fund for the children and they're letting mom's paws blow up. Then just a few years ago they gave a bonus, a considerable bonus to everybody in the province from their...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But people argued politics there and then?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh they just argued and just, just little groups like this you just get together and women and everybody talked. That was one of our main entertainments, main topics of conversation.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And here you found that absence?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: It was just complete blank. It was just as pure as the driven snow. Well they were all, they all thought Roosevelt was great but they didn't know why but it's better and that was it. They were happy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't know what did they talk about, what did Edwin talk about? Your dear cousin Edwin?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I don't know. Nothing, nothing, nothing. They had him in choosing and things like that. Give me an idea in your mind of why that difference was. Americans just don't have any political sense. I think there's this, no I don't think, I think it's just apathy. Just like they don't get us involved. And that's touching up there, it's the same game to be the same in Canada. But it didn't used to be. That's funny that there was that concern. I'm going to see what Jimmy Carter has got to say now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You ever read anything about him?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I don't know. I think he's got to be in some way. I'm going to vote for him. I don't want him to be as far as he's supposed to be. He's on the, as far as I can see now, he's on the table. And I can see without something new to himself. No, he's not. He's not good. We know what Ford's got. He's got the same old stuff only they got to work at from a different place. They was working over here last year, now they moved down here six inches and they're working from there. And they got maybe a few different words. I think here they're more interested in the everyday struggle. Get up wood, get your tractor and your machinery working.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where am I going to work next year?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I think I'll go out in the woods. They visited an awful lot. They were trailed back and forth between them, you know. This is just a different temple. I'm not saying it's right or wrong. It's just different.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But there, how would you contrast it? What would it have been like there as compared to this?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: There they were more tied into the government and what the government was doing. They were much more so. It's just no comparison. Everybody from the kids on, our parties, our dancers were to raise money, were to get so and so elected or to send a delegation or getting up a petition. Here we are just a handful of people and it isn't only us, it's all around us. They just, I think when the government after we left, I think that new one that was going in, I think he went in with something like 90 some percent of the vote. It's just overwhelming, the votes that went in. I don't think they can do that now. But you can imagine it's just a fervor. It's almost like a Billy Graham religious fervor when you get in. That's the way we were.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the party that everybody was for?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Most of them was for, he was called Eberhard. There was a U .F .A. There was a U .F. I was going to say U .F .O. In Canada now. There was United Farm Association.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now would anybody want to call it?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh no, no. You can get it later on. There was, United Farmers was in for a good number of years. And it did pretty good for the farmers. It was fine and when farming was good until the Depression hit then it went to pieces. And then, oh don't worry we have our little scandals. I think his name was Manning. Just about the time we were left, we left there, there was a man named Manning. He was the Prime Minister of Alberta. He got involved, I don't know if he was his secretary or whatever. But he had a wife, very highly respectable and boy he had something like Harding and his little butterfly that he had years ago. And oh, it was front page details and he was a whole year younger than him. Just barely legal. And there was no child or anything involved but boy talk about a sex scandal, this pillar of righteousness. And then there was this Eberhard. Now he was a very shrewd cookie. He was a good politician. He stayed there just year after year after year. And he really introduced marvellous reforms and he got things out of the slump they were in. And he was something like on the line of Roosevelt down here. But there was never any help. No welfare, not a handout, no work for people like Peace Corps or WPA or anything. He didn't perhaps, I don't know how he managed but he was a marvellous manager and he spoke every Sunday on the radio to the people. Every Sunday he would speak. Now that is keeping in touch. And it's a small enough place that you could respond. I don't say everybody was 100 % for him but his morals were irreproachable and that's what they wanted after this idea. This guy having a roll in the hay while everybody else was papped, starved to death and all these troubles and miseries in the country. It just didn't go over well. And now he's been succeeded by men. Maybe it's Manning, the next guy, I don't know. There's another fellow's community. He's carrying on very much. But he's not 100. He hasn't got the majority the other fellow had. You see, it's picked out different. I guess you know a little bit about the system out there. It's much simpler. Like you'd have Democrats and you'd all get them all elected in and then you might say when they get there then they pick out one. Although you've got a pretty good idea who you're going to have. Yes, for a party leader. You bypass these electoral colleges, you know, bypass the delegates and all that sort of thing and the big conventions. It's much simpler.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So how about having a cup of coffee before we go?
